itineraries.title: Indonesia: Bali & Lombok — Ubud, Gili Islands and Kuta's Wild Coast
itineraries.subtitle: From Ubud's jungle temples to Uluwatu's sea cliffs, through Sidemen's rice terraces, the crystal waters of Gili Air and the untouched beaches of southern Lombok.
itineraries.notes: Itinerary combining Bali (6 nights) and Lombok/Gili (4 nights). D7 requires a speedboat from Amed to Gili Air (approx. 45 min to 1h30 depending on operator — book ahead). D9→D10 involves a domestic flight from Lombok (LOP) to Denpasar (DPS), approx. 30 min. Bring a sarong for all temple visits. Dry season recommended: May to October.

visits.text: Ubud is best explored on foot: start with Jalan Raya Ubud, the main street, lined with art galleries, batik shops and temples carved from volcanic stone. The Balinese call Ubud the 'navel of Bali' — it has been the island's artistic and spiritual centre for centuries. From early morning, you'll see locals placing banana-leaf offerings in front of every shop and shrine. Pack a sarong in your daypack: you'll need one for every temple today.

Pura Taman Saraswati is one of Ubud's most photographed temples, dedicated to the goddess of knowledge. Its lotus-covered pool catches the morning light with rare serenity. Just opposite, Pura Desa is the village's communal temple, dedicated to Brahma, the protector god. Don't stop at the façade: walk into the inner courtyard to see the daily offerings still fresh from dawn. Free admission — enter quietly and keep your shoulders covered.

Puri Saren Agung, Ubud's Royal Palace, sits at the heart of town. The Balinese royal family still lives here, but part of the complex is open to visitors. The architecture — paved courtyards, garuda sculptures and centuries-old frangipani trees — is a textbook example of classic Balinese style. Every evening from 7:30 PM, the courtyard becomes a stage for traditional Legong and Barong dances. Reserve your spot in the afternoon: performances often sell out in high season.

activities.text: The Subak Juwuk Manis Walk follows rice paddies irrigated by the traditional subak system, a UNESCO Cultural Landscape since 2012. This network of channels is managed collectively by the village farmers — if a channel clogs, everyone pitches in to fix it. The walk takes about an hour on foot from central Ubud. In the morning, low golden light rakes across the stepped terraces: the ideal moment to pull out your camera.

Dinner at a local warung around Jalan Dewisita or Jalan Bisma — these small side streets off the main road concentrate the best traditional Balinese kitchens. Order a nasi campur (rice with an assortment of local condiments) or a lawar, a ceremonial dish of minced meat and grated coconut. Avoid the restaurants on the main drag in high season: prices double and quality often drops.

visits.text: Get up early for the Campuhan Ridge Walk: the trail starts near Gunung Lebah temple, two kilometres from the town centre. The path follows a ridge between the Wos and Cerik rivers, through elephant-grass fields and fluorescent-green paddies. The 2-kilometre walk takes one to two hours depending on your pace. Leave before 8 AM — after 9, organised tour groups arrive in force and the atmosphere shifts completely. The morning light on the valley mist is worth the early start.

The Sacred Monkey Forest Sanctuary, known as Mandala Suci Wenara Wana, is home to more than 700 long-tailed macaques roaming freely across 12.5 hectares on the southern edge of Ubud. Three 14th-century temples built from volcanic stone are tucked deep in the forest. The monkeys are used to visitors but can turn aggressive if they spot food — keep bags and sunglasses out of reach. Allow an hour and a half to explore the different trails and shrines.

The Pasar Seni, the art market facing the Royal Palace, is the place to pick up textiles, wood carvings and locally made silver jewellery. Posted prices are starting points for negotiation — begin at half the asking price. Watch out for cheap batiks: a genuine hand-drawn batik (batik tulis) takes hours of work and can be recognised by the slight asymmetry of its patterns. Machine-printed imitations sell for a tenth of the price.

activities.text: Catch a Legong or Kecak dance performance at Puri Saren Agung, Ubud's Royal Palace. The Legong, performed by young dancers in golden costumes, tells episodes from the Ramayana. The Kecak — created in the 1930s by choreographer Walter Spies from a male trance ritual — features a chorus of dozens of men chanting 'cak-cak' with no instruments at all. Shows begin around 7:30 PM; arrive 20 minutes early for good seats.

Late in the evening, stroll the lamp-lit lanes around Jalan Monkey Forest and Jalan Hanoman. These streets are packed with galleries open late, cafés with terraces suspended over the valleys, and warungs where locals dine well past dark. The scent of incense from the evening offerings mingling with the first notes of a gamelan drifting from a nearby temple — that's the real face of Ubud after nightfall.

visits.text: The Tegallalang rice terraces are Bali's postcard landscape — 12 km north of Ubud, a 20-minute scooter ride. Listed as a UNESCO Cultural Landscape under the subak irrigation system, they illustrate perfectly the collective water management practised here for centuries. Go early, before 9 AM: the light is beautiful and the terraces still empty. After 10, guides and souvenir sellers take over. The marked path through the terraces costs a few thousand rupiah.

Pura Tirta Empul, 15 km northeast of Ubud (30 to 45 minutes), is one of the most sacred temples in Bali. Its name means 'holy spring' in Balinese. Founded in 926 AD under the Warmadewa dynasty, the temple is built around a natural spring considered blessed. According to legend, the god Indra struck the earth with his spear to create an elixir of life and save his warriors from the poison of the evil king Mayadenawa. Today, Balinese come to purify themselves under the 30 water jets feeding two rectangular pools — this is the Melukat ritual. Visitors can take part by wearing a special sarong available for hire on site. Avoid the 11 AM to 2 PM window, the busiest period.

A few minutes from Tirta Empul, Pura Gunung Kawi is an 11th-century rock-cut mausoleum carved into the cliff face of the Pakerisan gorge. Ten shrines sculpted in high relief are dedicated to the kings of the Warmadewa dynasty. To reach them, descend 350 steps through terraced rice paddies — the climb back up will work up a sweat, but the sight is worth it. Arrive before 10 AM to avoid the crowds.

activities.text: Stop for lunch on the way back near Sari Organik or at a warung perched above the Tegallalang terraces. These small open-terrace restaurants overlook the green tiers as far as the eye can see — look for those with a genuine view over the lower terraces, as some only face the road. Nasi goreng or gado-gado (vegetables in peanut sauce) are the essential local dishes. Allow about an hour on site to soak up the panorama.

On the road between Ubud and Tegallalang, stop at Goa Gajah — the 'Elephant Cave' — a listed 9th-century archaeological site. The entrance to the sanctuary is a demon's mouth sculpted on a grand scale that you walk through to enter the cave. On site, a local Balinese guide can explain the ongoing ritual practices for around 150,000 rupiah — an hour of direct cultural transmission that transforms the experience entirely.

visits.text: The drive from Ubud to Sidemen (35 km, 1h to 1h15) winds through the hills of eastern Bali with sweeping views of volcanoes and rice paddies. Sidemen sits in the Karangasem regency, in a valley carved by the waters of Gunung Agung, the sacred volcano rising to 3,142 metres just 15 km away. Settle into your accommodation — in this village it almost invariably looks out over green terraces with the volcanic cone as a backdrop, one of the finest views in all of Bali.

In the afternoon, explore the Sidemen valley on foot or by scooter. The Sidemen Rice Field Trekking trail is a marked 3-kilometre path (entry: about 25,000 rupiah) that follows terraced rice paddies irrigated by subak channels. You'll pass women harvesting rice by hand — the terraces may be muddy, green or golden depending on the season. Ask the trail attendant to point you to the second, free path on higher ground, which offers a different angle on the lower terraces.

In the late afternoon, climb to Pura Bukit Sangkan Gunung, a temple perched on a hill above Sidemen, reachable in 10 minutes on foot from the village via a steep staircase. Not a tourist in sight — just an elevated view over the valley and centuries-old banyans in the silence. At first light or just before sunset, the low-angle light on the paddies is stunning from up here. Free entry — a small offering in the box is appreciated.

activities.text: Visit an ikat weaving workshop — Sidemen is known as Bali's village of weavers. Ikat (from the Indonesian word meaning 'to tie, to bind') is a cotton cloth with geometric patterns created by dyeing the yarn at precise intervals before weaving. The artisans work on traditional looms, passing the craft from mother to daughter. Songket, the more precious version, is woven with gold or silver thread and reserved for major religious ceremonies. The workshops let you buy quality pieces directly, cutting out the middlemen.

Sunset dinner at one of the warungs perched on the valley hillsides. The tables are often set on open bamboo terraces facing the cascading terraces and Mount Agung. The end-of-day light gilds the paddies in an orange glow that photographers and painters have been trying to capture for decades — Sidemen has attracted artists from around the world since the 1930s. Arrive 30 minutes before sunset to pick your spot.

visits.text: An essential day trip from Sidemen: Pura Besakih, 30 to 35 minutes by car. Perched at 1,000 metres on the slopes of Mount Agung, it's Bali's largest temple complex — more than 86 individual temples including 18 major ones, arranged on tiered terraces. Founded according to legend in the 8th century by the sage Rsi Markandeya, the site has been supported by the Balinese state since the 15th century and hosts more than 70 religious ceremonies a year. Arrive early in the morning to avoid the coach groups; queues can be long in season. Arrange a driver to wait: there are virtually no taxis available for the return.

activities.text: Rafting or trekking on the Telaga Waja river — the main waterway fed by the slopes of Mount Agung, flowing through the valley below Sidemen. Local operators run raft trips suitable for all levels over roughly 16 km of rapids and forested gorges, with some technical passages in the wet season. It's a more physical alternative to the paddy walks, with a perspective on east Bali's dense vegetation from water level.

In the late afternoon, head to Gembleng Waterfall, a cascade reached after a 15- to 20-minute walk from the road. The water tumbles into natural rock pools — perfect for cooling off after a day of sightseeing. The spot is far less crowded than the waterfalls near Ubud. Visit after the rainy season (May to October) for a more generous flow.

visits.text: The drive from Sidemen to Amed (60 km, about 2 hours) follows Bali's east coast with views across the Lombok Strait and the volcanic island of Lombok in the background. Stop at the Karangasem water palaces along the way: Tirta Gangga, built in 1948 by the last raja of Karangasem, is a complex of Balinese pools and fountains set among the rice paddies. Then Taman Ujung, even more impressive, spread out over the water and facing the sea — partly destroyed by the eruptions of Mount Agung in 1963 and since rebuilt. Allow 45 minutes at each site.

Arrival in Amed, a fishing village on Bali's northeast coast. The beach is made of pebbles and black volcanic sand — no white tropical sand here, but an authenticity you won't find in the south of the island any more. Every morning, the jukungs (colourful traditional fishing boats) return with the day's catch. Jemeluk Bay, five minutes from Amed, is the best shore-snorkelling spot: strap on your fins and swim 50 metres out to find colourful corals and your first turtles.

activities.text: Don't miss the trip to Tulamben, 20 minutes west of Amed, to dive or snorkel the USAT Liberty. This 120-metre American military cargo ship was torpedoed by a Japanese submarine on 11 January 1942 in the Lombok Strait, then beached at Tulamben. It sat there for 21 years before being swept into the sea by the eruption of Mount Agung in 1963. The wreck lies between 5 and 28 metres deep, just 30 metres from shore — accessible directly from the beach without a boat. Entirely colonised by corals over 60 years, it now shelters sea fans several metres across, barracuda, turtles and pygmy seahorses. Open to all levels, from beginner to expert.

At sunset, settle at the water's edge in Amed and watch the show: Mount Agung, often capped in cloud, turns red to the west, while the waters of the bay shift to violet. The small beachfront restaurants serve fish grilled to order — order early, the service is slow but the freshness of the catch more than makes up for it.

visits.text: The speedboat crossing from Amed to Gili Air takes between 45 minutes and an hour and a half depending on the operator. Book in advance — seats fill up fast in high season. On arrival, the contrast with Bali is instant: no motorised vehicles on the island. You get around on foot, by bicycle or by cidomo (horse-drawn cart). Gili Air is the closest of the three Gili islands to Lombok and the quietest — the right balance between the bustle of Gili Trawangan and the near-emptiness of Gili Meno. Drop your bags and walk the island loop in an hour and a half.

The 'Turtle Spot' is on the east coast of the island, marked by small wooden signs along the beach. Swim about ten metres from the shore to the seagrass beds where green and hawksbill turtles come to feed. Visibility in the water averages 20 to 30 metres. Don't touch them and stay 2–3 metres away when they surface to breathe. Best time is in the morning at high tide — after 11 AM, small jellyfish may appear on the north coast.

activities.text: Free snorkelling from the east and north coast beaches — the coral reefs start just a few dozen metres from shore. The stretch between the Bel Air Resort and the Sunrise Resort (southeast coast) is reputed for the density of turtles. Rent a mask, snorkel and fins from any bar or restaurant on the island for a few euros. If the weather is good and visibility cooperates, you'll probably spot turtles without needing an organised excursion.

practical_info.materiel: Mask + snorkel + fins for hire on the island

activities.text: Sunset from Gili Air's west beach, facing the open sea and the silhouettes of Bali's volcanoes on the horizon. The beach bars set out cushions and candles on the sand from 5:30 PM — order a coconut cocktail or a fresh juice and watch the sky blaze orange and pink. If you're lucky, the outline of Gunung Agung will appear between the clouds on the western horizon.

visits.text: Public boat from Gili Air to Bangsal (Lombok's port), then road to Kuta Lombok (roughly 90 km, 2 hours' drive). Kuta Lombok has nothing in common with its overcrowded Balinese namesake. Here, fishermen still land on near-empty beaches and the village lanes keep an end-of-the-world feel. Kuta's main beach is tucked into a crescent bay — its sand has a distinctive grainy texture that earns it the local nickname 'pepper sand.' Order a coconut and watch the boats come and go.

Tanjung Aan bay is five minutes east of Kuta: two coves separated by a rocky headland, one with very fine white sand, the other with faintly pink tones from coral fragments mixed into the sand. The turquoise water is remarkably clear. On a weekday outside peak season, you can have the place virtually to yourself. In high season (July–August), arrive when the car parks open. Pantai Seger, just to the west, is even wilder and worth a detour.

Mawun Beach is 15 minutes west of Kuta: a perfect crescent bay entirely enclosed by bare hills that cut the wind and flatten the sea. The water is calm, ideal for swimming, and the spot stays uncrowded outside weekends. From the hilltop overlooking the beach to the north, the view down over the bay and surrounding ridges is worth the 10-minute walk up.

activities.text: Panoramic hike up Merese Hill — the headland overlooking Tanjung Aan east of Kuta. The climb takes 20 minutes from the Tanjung Aan car park on a well-marked trail. The summit delivers a 360° view over both coves of the bay, the green inland hills and the Indian Ocean horizon. It's one of the best sunset spots in southern Lombok — arrive an hour beforehand to pick your rock.

Dinner at a local warung in Kuta Lombok — the street-food options are far better and cheaper than the tourist restaurants along the waterfront. Ayam taliwang (grilled chicken with spices and Lombok sambal, hotter than the Balinese versions) and plecing kangkung (stir-fried water spinach in tomato-chilli sauce) are the island's signature dishes. The Sasak eat spicy — flag your tolerance level before ordering.

visits.text: Selong Belanak is 20 km west of Kuta (30 minutes on a paved road). It's a long blonde-sand beach curving into an almost enclosed bay — the water is gentle, ideal for family swimming or learning to surf. Operators on the beach hire out foam boards for beginners. The fishing village behind the dunes remains surprisingly untouched by development. Warungs serve freshly grilled fish right by the beach.

Mawi Beach is 30 minutes west of Kuta on a sometimes rough road (choose a scooter with decent ground clearance). The beach, framed by steep bare hills, is one of southern Lombok's most renowned surf breaks — powerful waves, for experienced surfers only. But even without a board, the bay is worth the trip: wild, barely developed, with a late-morning light that turns the water jade. Bring water — there's virtually no infrastructure on site.

The village of Sade, 15 minutes from Kuta (near Rambitan), is one of the best-preserved Sasak villages on Lombok. The Sasak people, Lombok's indigenous Muslim ethnic group, still live in traditional houses called 'bale,' built from woven bamboo with rumbia-leaf roofs. The villagers carry on the ancestral technique of weaving songket — a colourful cloth with gold or silver thread reserved for ceremonies. A visit with a village resident is far more illuminating than the bus tours.

activities.text: In the evening, head to Kuta Lombok's night market — a few streets in the centre come alive from 6 PM with stalls selling grilled skewers, fresh juices and local crafts. Ayam taliwang (grilled chicken with spices and Lombok sambal, hotter than the Balinese version) and plecing kangkung (stir-fried water spinach in tomato-chilli sauce) are the dishes to try. The Sasak eat spicy — flag your tolerance level before ordering.

In the late afternoon, sunset over Tanjung Aan bay from the Merese Hill promontory or from the beach itself. The water shifts from turquoise to copper, water buffalo file home from the fields along the coastal road and fishermen ready their boats for the night. This is the everyday tableau of southern Lombok — simple and powerful. Last evening here: make the most of it before tomorrow's flight.

visits.text: Morning flight from Lombok (LOP) to Denpasar (DPS), duration approx. 30 minutes. From the airport, head straight to the Bukit peninsula (30 min south) for a half-day of exploring before sunset. Rent a scooter to get around the limestone plateau — the roads are well paved and signposted. Start with Padang Padang, wedged between two cliffs and reached down 150 steps: a natural turquoise pool ringed by boulders. Continue to Bingin Beach, quieter and perfect for a lunch stop facing the waves.

Pura Luhur Uluwatu perches 70 metres above the Indian Ocean on a cliff at the southwest tip of the Bukit. Founded in the 11th century by the sage Mpu Kuturan, it is one of Bali's six 'spiritual pillar' temples, dedicated to the spirits of the sea. The walk along the coastal path around the temple is spectacular: you see the building from below against the cliffs, with waves crashing 70 metres beneath you. The temple macaques are notorious pickpockets — stow sunglasses and jewellery before entering. Arrive at least an hour and a half before sunset to explore the site at leisure.

activities.text: The Uluwatu Kecak dance takes place every evening at 6 PM on an open-air stage clinging to the cliff, with the ocean as a backdrop. The Kecak was created in the 1930s by Walter Spies, a German painter living in Bali, who drew on the male trance ritual Sanghyang to develop this choral spectacle. Several dozen men seated in a circle chant 'cak-cak-cak' with no instruments at all — the only accompaniment — while costumed dancers perform scenes from the Ramayana as the sun sinks into the ocean. Buy your ticket on arrival; get there 30 minutes early for good seats in the tiers.

Seafood dinner on Jimbaran Beach, 20 minutes north of Uluwatu. Dozens of plastic tables and chairs are set directly on the sand at the water's edge, and vendors offer fish, prawns and squid grilled to order. It's the perfect end-of-trip ritual: feet in the sand, the lights of Jimbaran Bay twinkling in the distance and the skewers still smoking in front of you. Arrive before 7 PM for a good spot.

seo.h1_title: Indonesia: Bali & Lombok — Ubud, Gili Islands and Kuta's Wild Coast
seo.intro_paragraph: Start from Ubud, Bali's spiritual capital, and cross the hidden Sidemen valley with its rice terraces under the gaze of Mount Agung. Reach Amed and its dives on the USAT Liberty wreck torpedoed in 1942, then catch the speedboat to Gili Air and its free-roaming sea turtles. Finish on the virgin beaches of southern Lombok and the Uluwatu cliffs with the Kecak dance at sunset.
seo.seo_hook: Hindu temples buried in the jungle, a wreck colonised by coral, turtles 30 metres from the beach and 70-metre cliffs above the Indian Ocean — Bali and Lombok at their best in 10 days.
featured_list: Ubud: Bali's artistic and spiritual capital for centuries
Gili Air: free-roaming turtles and coral reefs within snorkelling distance
Uluwatu: a temple perched on a 70-metre cliff, with the Kecak dance at sunset
Lombok: the unspoilt beaches of Selong Belanak and a preserved Sasak culture

places_taglines.ID::ubud: Bali's artistic and spiritual heart, where Hindu temples and UNESCO rice terraces merge into the jungle
places_taglines.ID::sidemen: A hidden valley in east Bali, where rice terraces glow beneath Mount Agung and ancestral ikat looms still click
places_taglines.ID::amed: A black-sand fishing village where you snorkel a WWII shipwreck at Tulamben without leaving shore
places_taglines.ID::gili-air: A car-free speck between Bali and Lombok, with sea turtles grazing in the shallows
places_taglines.ID::kuta-lombok: Wild southern beaches, Mawi's surf break and the traditional Sasak village of Sade
places_taglines.ID::uluwatu: A sacred cliff temple 70 metres above the Indian Ocean, where the Kecak chant meets the sunset

highlights: Pura Tirta Empul: purification temple founded in 926 AD, Melukat ritual under 30 sacred water jets
USAT Liberty at Tulamben: 120 m cargo ship torpedoed in 1942, now a coral-encrusted artificial reef between 5 and 28 m
Gili Air: the only car-free Gili island, with green and hawksbill turtles 30 m from shore year-round
Pura Luhur Uluwatu: cliff temple at 70 m, one of Bali's 6 spiritual pillars, nightly Kecak dance since the 1930s

itineraries.title: Bali in 10 Days: From the Trendy South to the Hidden Rice Terraces of the East
itineraries.subtitle: Seminyak beaches, Uluwatu cliffs, Ubud temples, Munduk crater lakes and Sidemen rice terraces — the complete tour of the Island of the Gods
itineraries.notes: Land-based Bali itinerary only. No sea transfers required. Return from Sidemen to Denpasar Airport (DPS) on Day 10 — allow about 2 hours of driving. The Ubud–Munduk road crosses the northern highlands (Bedugul): winding route, best avoided at night.

map_keywords: Eat Street Jalan Kayu Aya Seminyak

visits.text: Welcome to Bali — your first stop is Seminyak, the trendiest stretch of the island's south coast. From Ngurah Rai Airport (DPS), it's about a 30-minute drive into a long coastal strip where five-star hotels rub shoulders with designer boutiques. Start with a stroll along the beach: the sand is golden grey, the waves moderate — the sea here works well for beginner surfers, though watch out for currents at low tide. As the sun drops, beach clubs come alive and terraces fill up — this is the Seminyak show in full swing.

Just steps from the beach, don't walk past Pura Petitenget without stopping. This 16th-century temple is one of six sacred sea temples forming a 'spiritual chain' of protection around the island, established by the great Hindu priest Dang Hyang Nirartha during his pilgrimage from Java. Its name means 'magic box' — according to legend, it contained the priest's powers. The red brick and sandstone architecture with its dragon-carved stairway is worth a look. Bring a sarong to enter (mandatory; available at the entrance). Arrive early in the morning to avoid the weekend crowds.

Double Six Beach (Pantai Berawa) is the quintessential sunset spot in Seminyak. Colourful loungers line the waterfront, beach bars start turning up the volume around 5 pm and locals join tourists to watch the orange disc sink into the ocean. It's the most social stretch of the area — arrive about an hour before sunset to grab a spot.

Legian, squeezed between Kuta and Seminyak, is often overlooked even though it deserves a good 30 minutes of wandering. Its side streets running perpendicular to the beach are lined with Balinese painting galleries, sculpture shops and sarong tailors. The vibe sits somewhere between Kuta's bazaar energy and Seminyak's polish — and so do the prices. A solid stop for mid-range souvenir shopping.

Kuta Beach, 3 km south of Seminyak, is the birthplace of Bali tourism. Since the 1930s, Australian and American surfers set down their boards here and gradually put the island on the global travel map. Today the beach stretches over 3 km, is wide, and the surf remains excellent for beginners thanks to the sandy bottom and moderate waves. The Kuta neighbourhood has lost in charm what it gained in hotels, but the beach itself is still very much there.

activities.text: In the evening, head to Jalan Kayu Aya, dubbed 'Eat Street' by locals. This pedestrian road packs in many of Seminyak's best restaurants: traditional Balinese cuisine, fresh seafood, fusion spots. Grab a terrace table and order a nasi campur (rice with assorted sides) or a sate lilit (Balinese spiced fish skewer) for your first taste of the local food scene.

visits.text: The beaches of Seminyak and Kuta are really one continuous stretch of sand running over 5 kilometres. Since the 1930s, American and Australian surfers have been riding the waves at Kuta Beach — historically one of the cradles of surfing in Asia. Today, the Seminyak section concentrates the beach clubs and luxury hotels, while Kuta remains more popular and affordable. The dry season (April–October) is ideal for surfing: offshore winds from the northeast give the waves their shape. In the wet season, onshore winds degrade conditions and debris can wash up on the beach.

If shopping appeals, Seminyak Square and the surrounding craft galleries are worth an hour of browsing. You'll find Balinese sarongs, ceramics, wood carvings and silver jewellery from Celuk. Watch out for listed prices: bargaining is expected in small shops, though not in branded stores.

In Denpasar, Pasar Badung is Bali's largest traditional market. Four floors overflow with spices, flowers for offerings, exotic fruits, sarongs and kitchen utensils. This is where Balinese cooks stock up every morning from 4 am. The market is loud, dense and colourful — nothing like a tourist-oriented market. Go early in the morning and wander without a set agenda.

Canggu, a few kilometres north of Seminyak, is the neighbourhood that overtook Kuta as Bali's hip epicentre since the pandemic. Batu Bolong Beach, with its black volcanic sand and consistent waves, has become one of the most popular surf spots in the south of the island. Cafés, vegan restaurants and street-art galleries line the lanes — the atmosphere is a world apart from Seminyak: more bohemian, less flashy.

Petitenget Beach, directly in front of the temple of the same name, is the quietest stretch in the Seminyak area. Fewer beach clubs, more Balinese families strolling at the end of the day, a handful of fishermen pulling in their nets. A useful contrast to step outside the hotel bubble and see how locals live along their own coastline.

activities.text: Use your second day to try a surf lesson. Seminyak's conditions — sandy bottom, short and shallow waves — make it one of the best beginner spots in Bali. Operators offer 90-minute group lessons on the beach with a foam board and instruction. Allow 2 hours on site so you don't miss the incoming-tide session, which offers the best conditions.

practical_info.booking: Operators available directly on the beach

activities.text: In the late afternoon, treat yourself to a traditional Balinese massage: an age-old technique combining thumb pressure, stretching and application of herb-infused coconut oil. Seminyak has one of the highest concentrations of spas on the island. Allow 1 to 1.5 hours for a full session.

map_keywords: Jimbaran Bay seafood restaurants Muaya Beach

visits.text: Pura Luhur Uluwatu sits perched on a 70-metre cliff above the Indian Ocean — one of the most spectacular views in all of Bali. This sea temple is one of the six 'Sad Kahyangan', the sacred directional temples that protect the island from negative forces according to Balinese Hindu tradition. Its history dates back at least to the 11th century, when Javanese monk Mpu Kuturan laid its earliest foundations. It was later expanded by Danghyang Nirartha in 1489, who, legend has it, reached spiritual enlightenment (moksha) here and vanished in a flash of light. The name Uluwatu comes from Balinese: ulu (edge) and watu (rock). Arrive at least an hour before sunset to walk the clifftop path along the bluffs. Hold on to your sunglasses: the long-tailed macaques — considered sacred guardians — have a nasty habit of snatching them.

On the way down to the coast, stop at Padang Padang Beach. This small cove tucked between limestone cliffs was made famous by the film Eat Pray Love. Access is through a narrow passage cut into the rock. The water is clear and the waves fairly powerful — it's more of an advanced surf break than a family swimming beach, but the views from above are worth the detour on their own.

activities.text: The Kecak is the most striking cultural performance in Bali, and Uluwatu is its ideal stage. Every evening at 6 pm, 50 to 70 bare-chested men form concentric circles in the cliff-edge amphitheatre and begin a rhythmic 'cak-cak-cak' chant — with no musical instruments at all. The piece tells the Ramayana: Prince Rama, his wife Shinta abducted by demon king Rahwana, and Hanuman the white monkey warrior who burns Rahwana's palace to free her. The fiery finale against the orange sunset sky is unforgettable. Book your seats in advance during peak season and arrive before 5 pm to get a good spot. Note: the amphitheatre is exposed to the sun until 6 pm — bring sunscreen.

practical_info.booking: Booking recommended in peak season

activities.text: After the show, head down to Jimbaran Bay for dinner with your feet in the sand. Before becoming a dining hotspot, Jimbaran was a simple fishing village. Today, around thirty seafood restaurants line the white sand of Pantai Muaya, their wooden tables just metres from the waves. The speciality: fresh fish grilled over coconut-husk charcoal, served with rice, sautéed water spinach and two varieties of sambal. Choose your fish at the counter by the entrance. The crowds arrive around 7 pm — try to get there earlier.

visits.text: The Tegalalang Rice Terraces, located 10 km north of central Ubud, are one of Bali's most iconic sights. Listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site as part of the subak irrigation system — whose origins trace back to the 8th century and the holy man Rsi Markandeya — they cascade in tiers down the slopes of a lush valley. Come early in the morning, before 9 am: tour buses arrive mid-morning and the place gets crowded fast. March and September offer the most intense green (just before harvest). Don't settle for the view from the road: head down into the valley on the footpaths to see the farmers at work.

The Ubud Monkey Forest, officially the Sacred Monkey Forest Sanctuary, is home to over 700 long-tailed macaques spread across a tropical woodland in the heart of town. The site includes three Hindu temples dating from the 14th century. The monkeys are used to visitors but remain wild — hold tight to your glasses, cameras and bags. A banana in your pocket will make you their best friend.

The Ubud Art Market (Pasar Seni) is the best place to pick up authentic souvenirs: wood carvings, Balinese paintings, endek and songket cloth (a ceremonial weave once reserved for Balinese nobility), jewellery and ritual objects. The market is open daily from dawn. Arrive early for the lowest prices and the thinnest crowds. The Ubud Royal Palace (Puri Saren Agung) sits right next to the market and is worth a stop.

In the late afternoon, treat yourself to the Campuhan Ridge Walk: a 2-to-3-km trail running along the ridge between two streams, crossing hills covered in grass and banana trees with wide-open views over the Balinese countryside. It's one of the few walks in Ubud where you don't have to pay an entrance fee. Set off around 5 pm for the golden light of late afternoon.

activities.text: In the evening, catch a traditional dance performance at Puri Saren Agung (Ubud Royal Palace). Several styles of Balinese dance are presented nightly in the palace courtyard: the Legong (a female dance with ultra-precise eye and finger movements), the Barong (a dramatisation of the battle between good and evil) and the Topeng (masked dance). Operators run sessions from 7:30 pm. Book on the spot or through your accommodation.

practical_info.booking: Tickets available at the entrance or via your accommodation

map_keywords: Goa Gajah Elephant Cave Temple Ubud

visits.text: Goa Gajah, the 'Elephant Cave', is one of Bali's most intriguing archaeological sites. The cave entrance is carved with a monstrous mouth — most likely a kala, a guardian demon from the Hindu-Buddhist tradition — which archaeologists date to the 11th century. Inside, a small chamber holds statues of Ganesh and Shiva lingams. In front of the cave, two 11th-century stone pools were rediscovered during excavations in the 1950s; the fountains are still used for ritual purification. Bring a sarong (provided at the entrance).

Tirta Empul, in Tampaksiring, is one of Bali's most sacred temples. It was founded in 962 according to Balinese chronicles, around a natural spring gushing from the ground — the Balinese believe its waters were created by the god Indra to resurrect his poisoned warriors. Today, hundreds of worshippers dressed in white immerse themselves daily in the purification pools. The sight is striking. Tourists can take part in the melukat (purification ritual) in appropriate clothing — if you want to bathe, bring a change of clothes. Arrive before 9 am to beat the groups.

Tegenungan is the most easily accessible waterfall from Ubud, about a 15-minute drive away. The 15-metre drop pours into a pool of black stones where swimming is possible. Tropical vegetation frames the site. Bring a change of clothes and walking shoes — the path down is slippery.

activities.text: Ubud is the Balinese capital of kopi luwak and arabica plantations. Operators around Ubud offer plantation tours where you discover how coffee, cocoa and vanilla are grown in volcanic soil — the ash from Mount Batur and Mount Agung gives the beans a unique mineral richness. You'll inevitably be introduced to the famous kopi luwak (made from beans digested by the civet cat): interesting to know about, but you're under no obligation to buy it. The tasting of the other varieties — ginger, lemongrass, cocoa — is included and worth the trip on its own.

In the evening, join a Balinese cooking class — one of the best ways to get to grips with the local culture. The class usually starts with a visit to the morning market to choose ingredients, then under the guidance of a local cook you prepare a series of traditional dishes: sate lilit (spiced fish skewers), sayur urab (coconut vegetable salad), lawar (spiced meat and vegetable mix) and of course nasi goreng. Operators run 3-to-4-hour sessions from central Ubud.

practical_info.booking: Booking recommended the day before

map_keywords: Mount Batur sunrise trek Kintamani Bali
Pura Ulun Danu Batur lake temple Bali
Toya Bungkah hot springs Lake Batur Bali
Toya Devasya hot springs Batur Bali

visits.text: Mount Batur is an active volcano standing 1,717 metres above a crater lake — one of the most striking panoramas in Bali. The sunrise trek is one of the island's most popular experiences, and with good reason: from the summit, you watch the sun emerge behind Mount Agung (Bali's highest peak at 3,031 m) as its rays light up the lake and the rice terraces below. Departure is around 3 am from the villages in the caldera. The climb takes roughly 2 hours on a volcanic ash trail. A local guide is mandatory (enforced by the village guide associations). Moderate fitness level required.

After the descent, the natural hot springs of the Batur caldera are the perfect reward. The thermal pools at Toya Bungkah (a lakeside village) or nearby developed sites offer geothermal pools at 30–40 °C, with direct views of the lake and the volcano. A breakfast overlooking Lake Batur in one of the village warungs rounds off the morning nicely.

Pura Ulun Danu Batur, on the edge of the crater lake at Toya Bungkah, is one of Bali's most sacred temples — dedicated to Dewi Danu, goddess of the waters, and governing the entire subak irrigation system on the island. The original complex was engulfed by a lava flow from Mount Batur in 1926 and entirely rebuilt in 1929 on the crater rim. Allow 45 minutes to walk around it.

The ridgetop road at Penelokan offers a breathtaking panorama of the Batur caldera: a 600-metre drop between the crater rim and the lake, with the cone of the active volcano in the centre — its last major eruption was in 2000. Numerous viewpoints allow you to pull over and take in the volcanic landscape of Bali. Morning is ideal, before the clouds build up.

activities.text: In the afternoon, drive through Kintamani to admire the panorama from the ridgetop road overlooking the caldera: 600 metres of elevation separate the crater rim from the lake, and on a clear day you can see all the way to Lombok to the east. The many panoramic restaurants lining the road serve Balinese buffet lunches with views of Mount Batur — it's touristy, but the view is the real deal.

map_keywords: Twin Lakes viewpoint Buyan Tamblingan Wanagiri Bali

visits.text: The road from Ubud to Munduk crosses the Bedugul highlands — as you climb, the scenery shifts: rice paddies give way to coffee, clove and banana plantations, and the temperature drops several degrees. Munduk is a village perched in the volcanic hills of northern Bali, at roughly 900 metres above sea level — a striking contrast with the heat of the south. It was once a plantation centre developed during the Dutch colonial era. Bring a layer: evenings get cool.

Your first stop: the viewpoint over the twin lakes of Buyan and Tamblingan, from the Wanagiri ridgetop road. These two crater lakes sit within the same volcanic caldera and were originally a single body of water before 1800, when a landslide split them apart. Lake Buyan covers roughly 490 hectares; Tamblingan is smaller but more mystical, ringed by dense virgin forest. From the viewpoint, Mount Agung appears in the background on clear days. Arrive at sunrise for the best visibility.

Pura Ulun Danu Tamblingan, partly swallowed by forest on the lake shore, is one of Bali's most hidden temples. Reachable by traditional dugout canoe or via a jungle trail, it is draped in moss and ancient fig trees. Locals come here for regular ceremonies and pilgrimages. The contrast between the temple's meditative atmosphere and the wild canopy surrounding it is unforgettable.

activities.text: Hike through the forest between the two lakes with a crossing by traditional dugout canoe on Lake Tamblingan. The trail passes through dense tropical forest, skirts half-swallowed temple ruins and emerges on the shore of Lake Buyan. The flat-bottomed canoe crossing is offered by local village guides. Allow 3 to 4 hours for the whole experience; start early in the morning.

practical_info.booking: Guides available at Munduk village

visits.text: Start the day by exploring Munduk village itself. This highland settlement still has several Dutch colonial wooden houses dating from the plantation era (late 19th – early 20th century) — a rare sight on Bali. The village produces cloves, arabica coffee, vanilla and strawberries. Munduk's small and authentic morning market is the ideal place to buy local spices directly from the growers.

The waterfalls around Munduk are among the least visited and most beautiful on the entire island. The most accessible is Munduk Waterfall, about a 2-km walk from the village. For a more committed loop, the Golden Valley waterfall trail strings together 4 waterfalls over a 2-hour hike through a forest of clove trees, coffee plants and tree ferns. The most spectacular is Banyumala Twin Waterfall, two parallel jets plunging into a swimmable pool. Wear closed shoes — the trails can be muddy.

Stop at one of the coffee plantations in the Munduk area. The volcanic soil and altitude provide ideal conditions for Balinese arabica. Some family-run plantations welcome visitors for a walkthrough of the production stages — from coffee cherry to cup — followed by a tasting. It's also a chance to try cold-brew iced coffee, a refreshing local speciality after a morning of walking.

activities.text: In the late afternoon, take time to wander through Munduk village itself. Its Dutch colonial wooden houses, quiet warungs and flower markets (a legacy of the colonial era) make a pleasant contrast with the hustle of the south. This is the authentic Bali of the highlands: locals tending their plots, landscapes that haven't been curated for tourists. Walk through the lanes towards the end of the day to catch a glimpse of everyday life.

map_keywords: Pura Besakih Mother Temple Mount Agung Bali

visits.text: Sidemen is one of Bali's best-kept secrets. This village is tucked into the eastern valley of Karangasem, about 35 km northeast of Ubud — an Ubud without the tour buses, with terraced rice paddies thrown in and Mount Agung (3,031 m) as a permanent backdrop. The valley is an intense green, criss-crossed with paths between plots, bamboo bridges and small neighbourhood temples (sanggah). Here, farmers still work their fields by hand using the ancestral subak system. Set out on a walk from 7 am to see Agung before the clouds roll in.

Sidemen has been one of Bali's great textile weaving centres for centuries. Local artisans craft songket (a ceremonial cloth woven with gold and silver threads, once reserved for Balinese nobility) and gringsing, a complex double-ikat weave found only in the nearby village of Tenganan. Workshops let you watch the looms in action and try your hand at weaving for a few hours. Allow some time: certain fabrics take weeks to complete.

Pura Besakih, a 30-to-40-minute drive from Sidemen, is the largest and most sacred Hindu temple complex in Bali — the Balinese call it the 'Mother Temple' (Pura Agung). It comprises 86 individual temples terraced across the slopes of Mount Agung. Some foundation elements of the site may date back to the 8th century. The main complex is built on six stacked terraces, and the view over the valley from the top is vertiginous. Arrive early in the morning to beat the clouds around the volcano and the tour groups. A sarong is mandatory and respectful attire required.

activities.text: For waterfall lovers, Gembleng Waterfall, 7 km from Sidemen, is one of the most authentic in eastern Bali. Locals also use it for purification ceremonies (melukat). The access path runs alongside rice fields and gardens. Check the day before whether it's open: the waterfall is occasionally closed temporarily during religious ceremonies.

map_keywords: Lempuyang Temple Gates of Heaven Bali
Virgin Beach White Sand Beach Candidasa Bali

visits.text: If your flight is in the evening, the day allows you to explore the Sidemen area before heading back to Denpasar. Start with the 'Gates of Heaven' (Candi Bentar) at Lempuyang Temple, about an hour's drive away. This mountain temple perched on the lower slopes of Mount Lempuyang frames Bali's most photographed image: the split gateway perfectly framing the cone of Mount Agung. Arrive before 7 am to avoid the queue of photographers and the heat haze — the wait can exceed 2 hours in peak season.

Nearby, the Tirta Gangga Water Palace in Amlapura was built in 1946 by the Raja of Karangasem. Its cascading pools with deity-shaped fountains, giant koi and statues from Balinese mythology create a timeless setting. A deep calm reigns here, the polar opposite of the busy tourist sites down south. You can swim in the sacred pools in appropriate attire.

On the road back to Denpasar for your flight, stop at Virgin Beach (Pantai Pasir Putih) near Candidasa — one of the rare white-sand beaches in eastern Bali. Jungle-covered cliffs, shallow turquoise water, a handful of cold-drink vendors and barely any tourists: this is the Bali of 20 years ago. The drive from here to Denpasar Airport takes roughly 1.5 to 2 hours depending on traffic.

activities.text: One last possible morning in the Sidemen rice terraces before hitting the road. Walk up the ridgeline path overlooking the valley on its eastern side for a full view of Mount Agung — on a clear day, the panorama stretches across the terraces, down the valley and out to the Lombok ocean on the horizon. A fine farewell to end the trip on.

seo.h1_title: Bali in 10 Days: From the Trendy South to the Hidden Rice Terraces of the East
seo.intro_paragraph: Ten days to cross Bali end to end: Seminyak's beach clubs, the sacred cliffs of Uluwatu with its sunset Kecak, Ubud's temples and rice terraces, the misty crater lakes of Munduk in the north, and finally the quiet valley of Sidemen in the shadow of Mount Agung. Bali in all its forms, far from the clichés.
seo.seo_hook: Uluwatu cliffs and Kecak at sunset, UNESCO rice terraces in Ubud, volcanic lakes at Munduk, Mount Agung from Sidemen — Bali, the grand-tour edition.
featured_list: Uluwatu: clifftop temple and sunset Kecak performance
Ubud: UNESCO-listed rice terraces dating back to the 8th-century subak system
Munduk: twin crater lakes separated by a landslide in 1800
Sidemen: hidden valley beneath Bali's tallest volcano

places_taglines.ID::seminyak: Trendy seaside strip where surf and beach clubs set the pace at sundown
places_taglines.ID::uluwatu: An 11th-century sea temple perched on a 70-metre cliff with nightly Kecak performances
places_taglines.ID::ubud: Bali's cultural heartbeat — UNESCO rice terraces and Hindu temples deep in the jungle
places_taglines.ID::munduk: A colonial mountain village ringed by volcanic lakes and coffee plantations
places_taglines.ID::sidemen: A hidden eastern valley of rice terraces, artisan weaving and views of Mount Agung

highlights: The Kecak at sunset in Uluwatu: 50 to 70 men in a circle narrating the Ramayana without instruments, facing the ocean
The Tegalalang Rice Terraces, UNESCO-listed as part of the 8th-century subak irrigation system
The twin lakes of Buyan and Tamblingan at Munduk: two crater lakes separated by a landslide in 1800
The Sidemen Valley with a direct view of Pura Besakih and Mount Agung (3,031 m), Bali's sacred volcano

itineraries.title: Java and Bali: Temples, Volcanoes and Rice Terraces in 14 Days
itineraries.subtitle: From Yogyakarta to Uluwatu — a crossing of Indonesia's two most iconic islands, with UNESCO temples, active volcanoes and terraced rice paddies.
itineraries.notes: Java → Bali itinerary. Java–Bali crossing by ferry from Banyuwangi (Ketapang) to Gilimanuk, about 45 min. The Kawah Ijen segment requires a very early start (2–3 am). The site is closed on the first Friday of every month. Bring a gas mask for the descent into the Ijen crater. Dry season recommended (April–October) for volcano visits.

visits.text: Borobudur sits on a hilltop at 265 metres above sea level, 40 km northwest of Yogyakarta — it is the largest Buddhist monument in the world. Built between 750 and 850 under the Sailendra dynasty, it rests on 55,000 m³ of andesite stone assembled without mortar and features 2,670 individual bas-relief panels covering 2,500 m² of carved surface. Its square base measures 123 metres per side and the central stupa rises to 35 metres. The temple was buried under volcanic ash from Mount Merapi for centuries before being rediscovered in 1814 by British governor Raffles, who sent 200 men to clear the jungle and expose it. Arrive before 7 am for sunrise over the stupas — after 9 am the tour groups descend and the site gets packed.

Two kilometres from Borobudur along the same spiritual axis, Mendut Temple houses one of the finest Buddha statues in all of Southeast Asia: a 3-metre-tall seated Buddha flanked by two bodhisattvas, carved from a single block of stone. Local legend holds that a walled, paved road once connected Mendut, the small Pawon temple and Borobudur into a single pilgrimage route. Allow 30 minutes on site — it's far less crowded than Borobudur and all the more serene for it.

activities.text: Set off on foot or by bicycle through the villages of the Kedu Plain at the foot of Borobudur: rice paddies, small neighbourhood temples, morning markets. This is one of the most fertile agricultural plains on Java, nicknamed 'the garden of Java' for centuries. If you're here in May or June, the Vesak (Waisak) celebrations bring thousands of Buddhist pilgrims who walk in procession from Mendut to Borobudur at the full moon.

visits.text: Prambanan is the Hindu counterpart to Borobudur: built around 850 AD by King Rakai Pikatan of the Sanjaya dynasty, this complex of 240 Shaivite temples is the largest Hindu ensemble in Indonesia. The main temple dedicated to Shiva stands 47 metres tall. Some historians see it as a direct response to Borobudur, erected a few decades earlier by the rival Buddhist dynasty — and remarkably, Rakai Pikatan's wife was the daughter of the king who had overseen Borobudur's construction. Head there in the late afternoon for the low-angled light on the spires, and if possible stay for the open-air Ramayana Ballet in the evening: 200 costumed dancers performing the epic with the temple as a backdrop.

Just 800 metres from Prambanan, Candi Sewu — 'the thousand temples' in Javanese — is Java's second-largest Buddhist complex after Borobudur. Its 249 towers, most of them in ruins, are guarded by imposing Dwarapala statues. The site sees very few visitors; take the time to wander through it — the atmosphere is far calmer than at its neighbour.

activities.text: If you have time at the end of the day, head up to the Ratu Boko site, a former royal palace perched on a plateau 200 metres above the plain. The ruins offer one of the best panoramas of Prambanan with Mount Merapi as a backdrop — come at sunset for the best effect.

visits.text: The Kraton — 'the place where the king lives' in Javanese — is far more than a palace: it's a city within a city, where 25,000 people still live behind 3-metre-high walls stretching 1 km on each side. Founded in 1755 by Sultan Hamengkubuwono I after the signing of the Treaty of Giyanti, which split the Mataram kingdom between Yogyakarta and Surakarta, the Kraton is today the residence of Sultan Hamengkubuwono X, who also serves as governor of the territory. Visit in the morning only — the palace is reserved for the family in the afternoon. The abdi dalem, servants who devote their lives to the sultan, quietly sweep the black-sand courtyards in traditional dress.

Steps from the Kraton, Taman Sari — 'flower garden' — is the former water castle built in 1758 by Hamengkubuwono I. Its pools served for the ablutions of the sultan's favourites: from a watchtower, the sovereign would choose which one would join him. Today this labyrinth of pools, arches and partially restored underground passages gives off a faintly mystical atmosphere. Legend even has it that a secret tunnel connects this place to Parangtritis beach, the abode of the goddess Nyai Loro Kidul.

Malioboro, Yogyakarta's central avenue, is the batik trading hub of all Java. If you're after quality hand-made batik, push through the doors of the workshops in the old town rather than the shops on the main avenue. The Kota Gede neighbourhood, to the southeast, is renowned for its silversmiths — it was the former capital of the first Mataram sultanate in the 16th century.

activities.text: In the evening, catch a wayang kulit performance — shadow-puppet theatre using leather puppets — or a gamelan show at the Kraton or one of the city's cultural centres. The Kraton holds performances on weekday mornings; ask your hotel about evening show schedules. This is one of the oldest living art forms on Java, and Yogyakarta is its undisputed capital.

visits.text: The road from Yogyakarta to Probolinggo runs along Java's southern flank, crossing rice paddies and small towns. From Probolinggo, make a detour if possible to Madakaripura Waterfall, hidden at the end of a sacred gorge: according to Javanese legend, this is where the 14th-century general Gajah Mada retreated to meditate before disappearing. The main waterfall drops 200 metres into a moss-covered amphitheatre of cliffs — you'll need to wade through the river for the last 200 metres. Bring trousers you can roll up.

activities.text: Probolinggo is the gateway to Mount Bromo. From the train station or bus terminal, take a minibus to Cemoro Lawang (about 2 hours of winding road through plantations). Settle into your accommodation early and get to bed before 10 pm: the alarm for sunrise goes off at 3 am.

visits.text: Wake-up at 3 am to reach the viewpoint at Mount Penanjakan (2,700 m) or King Kong Hill: at dawn, the Tengger caldera reveals itself beneath a sea of clouds. In the foreground, the perfect cone of Mount Batok (dormant); behind it, Bromo trails its permanent plume of smoke; in the background, Semeru — Java's highest point at 3,676 metres — erupts every 15 to 20 minutes. Bromo stands at 2,329 metres with a crater 800 metres across and 200 metres deep. From Cemoro Lawang, you can reach the viewpoint on foot in about 1 h 30 min in the dark — the quietest option to avoid the hundreds of jeeps.

After sunrise, descend into the Sea of Sand of the Tengger caldera — a vast expanse of volcanic ash and rock stretching 10 km across. At the foot of Bromo, the Hindu temple Pura Luhur Poten watches over ceremonies: the Tenggerese are one of the last Hindu ethnic groups on Java. Climb the 250 steps to the crater rim and peer into its smoking depths. Every year in July, during the Yadnya Kasada festival, the Tenggerese throw offerings into the volcano to appease the gods.

activities.text: In the afternoon, once the jeeps have left, the caldera returns to a striking silence. Walk through this near-lunar landscape, between dunes of volcanic sand and the small Mongolian-style horses grazing near the ridges. If you stay until evening, the late-afternoon light on the surrounding volcanoes is every bit as rewarding as the morning show.

visits.text: Kawah Ijen is one of only two places on earth where you can witness the blue-fire phenomenon — the other being in Iceland. It's not lava but sulphurous gases that ignite on contact with air above 220 °C, producing electric-blue flames up to 5 metres high. To see them, you must leave Paltuding by 2 am at the latest: the flames are only visible in darkness. The site officially opens at 4 am, but an earlier departure is possible with a guide. The crater also holds the most acidic lake in the world, with a pH of 0.2: the turquoise water is absolutely off-limits for swimming. Wear a gas mask — available for hire on site — especially for the descent into the crater.

As you climb back out of the crater at sunrise, you'll pass the sulphur porters descending and ascending several times a day carrying 70 to 90 kg of solidified sulphur on their shoulders. The sulphur, bright yellow to orange, is extracted from pipes at the lake's edge and sold to the sugar and rubber industries. It is one of the hardest jobs in the world: these miners have an estimated life expectancy of 40 to 50 years. A discreet tip is welcome if you photograph them.

activities.text: Late in the morning, after the climb, rest in Licin or head down to Banyuwangi to prepare for the ferry to Bali. Ketapang port is a 20-minute taxi ride from Banyuwangi; ferries to Gilimanuk depart every hour and the crossing takes about 45 minutes.

visits.text: The Ketapang–Gilimanuk ferry drops you at the western tip of Bali. The drive to Ubud (2.5 to 3 hours) crosses the heart of the island, skirting the rice paddies of the Tabanan plain. Ubud sits in the hills at 200 metres above sea level, in a green valley cut by two sacred rivers. If you arrive in the late afternoon, start with the Campuhan Ridge Walk: this 2-km trail follows a ridge between two river valleys with open views over the Balinese countryside. It's one of the rare spots in Ubud where you can easily escape the tourist flow.

activities.text: In the evening, catch a traditional Balinese dance performance: legong, barong or kecak. The Ubud Royal Palace (Puri Saren) programmes shows almost every night in its torch-lit courtyards. The legong is the most refined — codified gestures, gold-embroidered costumes, expressive gaze — centuries old and inscribed on UNESCO's Intangible Cultural Heritage list since 2015. Arrive 20 minutes early to get a good seat.

visits.text: Tirta Empul temple, founded in 926 AD under the Warmadewa dynasty, is one of the most sacred on Bali. Its name means 'gushing sacred spring' in Old Javanese. In the central courtyard, two purification pools fed by 30 carved spouts welcome Hindu Balinese pilgrims each morning who come to practise melukat — a purification ritual in which they pass under each jet in a precise order. Note that two of the 30 fountains are reserved for funerary rites. The temple is 30 minutes from Ubud towards Tampaksiring. Arrive early (before 9 am) to share the moment with locals rather than tour groups.

A few minutes from Tirta Empul, Gunung Kawi temple dates from the 11th century. You'll descend around a hundred steps through terraced rice paddies to reach ten sanctuaries carved directly into the cliff of the Pakrisan river — a setting of humid jungle and mossy stone that rivals Angkor. Less well known than Tirta Empul, it's often deserted outside group-tour hours.

The Tegalalang Rice Terraces, 10 km north of Ubud in the Gianyar regency, are inscribed on the UNESCO Intangible Heritage list. The subak irrigation system dates back to the 9th century, introduced by the sage Rsi Markandeya. The view is spectacular — coconut palms, emerald terraces stepping down the slope — but the place has become very touristy. The many Instagrammable structures (swings, nests) are pay-to-use. Stick to the farming paths between the plots for a more authentic experience.

activities.text: The Ubud Monkey Forest is home to over 600 long-tailed macaques in a 12-hectare grove that includes three Hindu temples dating from the 14th century. The monkeys roam completely free — stow your glasses, cameras and plastic bags before entering; they're particularly quick. The site is sacred to the Balinese, who come here regularly to pray at the temples.

visits.text: Sidemen sits in the Karangasem valley, in eastern Bali, beneath the silhouette of Mount Agung (3,031 m). Here, the brochure Bali gives way to the agricultural, living Bali: women carry colourful offerings on their heads each morning, subak irrigation channels wind between the houses, and terraced rice paddies tumble down to the Telaga Waja river. Walk the marked trails between the terraced plots — allow 1.5 to 2 hours for the main loop with views of Agung.

Pura Besakih, the 'Mother Temple' of Bali, is the island's largest Hindu temple complex: 86 temples and shrines terraced across the slopes of Mount Agung, to which the faithful attribute a history stretching back over 2,000 years. The temple is mentioned in texts from the 14th century. As you climb towards the main temple, Pura Penataran Agung, you pass through successive courtyards where incense burns, and the view over the caldera is striking. Arrive at opening (8 am) to avoid the fake guides, some of whom falsely claim that paid offerings are mandatory — they are not.

activities.text: The Telaga Waja river running through the Sidemen valley offers white-water rafting over roughly ten kilometres through jungle-covered gorges and waterfalls. Local operators run 2-to-3-hour descents from the village — it's gentler than the Ayung rapids near Ubud, suitable for all levels. Prefer the morning for cooler water.

visits.text: Tukad Cepung waterfall, about an hour from Sidemen, is hidden inside a gorge: you squeeze between two rock walls through a narrow passage and wade through the river to emerge in a natural chamber where the morning sun pierces the cliff face and illuminates the falls in an almost mystical beam of light. The best time is between 8 and 10 am for the right angle. Don't go if you're prone to claustrophobia — the passages are tight.

The village of Tenganan Pegeringsingan, not far from Candidasa, is one of the rare Bali Aga villages — the 'original Balinese' who resisted the Hindu-Javanese influence of the 14th century. They still weave geringsing here, a double-ikat cloth requiring several years to make and produced nowhere else in the world. Entry is free and the village sees few tourists: it's one of the most authentic spots in eastern Bali.

The Taman Ujung water palace (Sukasada), built in the early 20th century by the king of Karangasem, overlooks the sea with its infinity pools and ornamental pavilions. Partly destroyed by the 1979 earthquake before being restored, it offers a panoramic view over Bali's east coast and the neighbouring islands. Go in the late afternoon for the best light.

activities.text: Virgin Beach (Pantai Pasir Putih), tucked into a small cove 10 km from Candidasa, is one of the rare white-sand beaches in eastern Bali. Less crowded than the southern beaches, it's a perfect stop for a midday swim before heading back up to Sidemen.

map_keywords: Pura Ulun Danu Bratan Lake Bedugul

visits.text: Munduk sits at 900 metres above sea level in the mountains of northern Bali, surrounded by coffee, cocoa and clove plantations. The village is the starting point for a 15-metre waterfall reachable on foot in 20 minutes from the centre — cold water and natural pool included. Further along the same trail, Banyu Wana Amertha waterfall actually comprises 4 successive falls, each quite different from the last. Bring closed shoes and clothes you don't mind getting wet.

The floating temple Pura Ulun Danu Bratan rests on the waters of Lake Bratan at 1,200 metres above sea level, inside a former volcanic caldera holding three lakes — Bratan, Buyan and Tamblingan. Built in the 17th century under the reign of the King of Mengwi, it is dedicated to Dewi Danu, goddess of lakes and rivers, and plays a central role in the traditional Balinese irrigation system. Its tiered roofs (meru) reflect on the water in a perfect mirror when the lake is calm — go early in the morning before the fog sets in.

activities.text: From the panoramic road running along lakes Buyan and Tamblingan, a free viewpoint takes in the twin lakes separated by a thin strip of vegetation — they were a single lake until the 19th century, when a landslide split them apart. A little further on, the Banjar hot springs offer three pools of sulphurous water at 37 °C, fed by dragon-head-shaped fountains: perfect after a morning of hiking.

map_keywords: Pura Tanah Lot Temple Bali sunset

visits.text: Pura Tanah Lot sits on an isolated rock in the sea, 30 minutes north of Seminyak. It is one of Bali's seven sea temples — the Balinese believe these temples form a belt of spiritual protection around the island. Founded in the 16th century by Javanese priest Dang Hyang Nirartha during his pilgrimage to Bali, the temple is only accessible at low tide. Come at sunset for the temple's silhouette against a red sky — it's one of the most photographed images in Indonesia. Arrive an hour early to find a good spot.

On Seminyak beach, Pura Petitenget is a discreet but very active temple: it's one of the links in the spiritual chain connecting Tanah Lot in the south to Uluwatu — ceremonies regularly set off from here towards the beach, with offerings entrusted to the waves at sunset. Visit on a Friday evening for the best chance of witnessing a ceremony.

activities.text: Canggu beach, 10 km north of Seminyak, is home turf for surfers of all levels. Echo Beach's waves suit intermediates, while Batu Bolong's are right for beginners. Local operators offer 2-hour lessons. If you don't surf, settle into one of the beach clubs overlooking the ocean to watch the surfers at sunset.

map_keywords: Jatiluwih Rice Terraces UNESCO Tabanan Bali

visits.text: The Jatiluwih Rice Terraces, a UNESCO World Heritage Site (subak irrigation system), are the largest and least touristy on Bali. They cover over 600 hectares on the flanks of Mount Batukaru at 700 metres above sea level. Unlike Tegalalang, there are no swings or pay-to-photograph spots here: just 15 km of farming paths winding through the terraces, with the mountain forest and the distant peak of Agung as a backdrop. Go before 10 am to beat the heat and catch the east-facing morning light.

On the way to Jatiluwih, stop at Pura Luhur Batukaru: nestled in dense tropical forest on the slopes of Mount Batukaru (2,276 m), it is one of the oldest and most sacred temples in Bali, far less crowded than Besakih. The atmosphere is lush and mysterious, with tree ferns and orchids growing on the mossy stone walls. A sacred pool fed by a mountain spring adorns the entrance.

activities.text: In the evening in Seminyak, Jalan Oberoi and Jalan Kayu Aya concentrate the Balinese designer boutiques (batik, furniture, silver jewellery) and the restaurants. This is where Bali's luxury tourism took off in the 1990s. The beachfront beach clubs offer sunset sessions with a DJ — it's touristy, but the ocean show is the real thing.

visits.text: Pura Luhur Uluwatu is perched 70 metres above the Indian Ocean on the southwest tip of the Bukit peninsula. Founded in the 11th century by the Javanese sage Mpu Kuturan, it is one of Bali's six spiritual pillar temples (sad kahyangan), charged with protecting the island from harmful influences from the sea. Its petrified-coral blocks take on golden hues at sunset. Watch out for the colony of monkeys living within the grounds — they snatch sunglasses, bags and devices with remarkable efficiency. Arrive by 4 pm to enjoy the temple before the sunset crowds.

The Uluwatu Kecak dance is a performance created in the 1930s by choreographer Walter Spies from an old trance ritual called Sanghyang. A chorus of 50 to 70 men chants 'cak' in rhythm — with no instruments at all — while dancers enact scenes from the Ramayana. The show is held outdoors on the cliff at 6 pm and 7 pm, with the sun setting over the ocean as a backdrop. Book your seats in advance during peak season — the stands fill up fast.

activities.text: Padang Padang beach, wedged into a crack between two cliffs, is one of the best-known beaches on the Bukit peninsula — it served as a filming location for Eat, Pray, Love. Reached by stairs cut into the rock, it's tiny at high tide but perfect for watching local surfers ride the waves. Allow a 30-minute drive from Uluwatu. Before your flight, the fishermen's restaurants on Jimbaran Bay serve grilled-seafood platters on the sand, under the lights of the bay. The vibe is casual and local — arrive early to pick your table.

seo.h1_title: Java and Bali: Temples, Volcanoes and Rice Terraces in 14 Days
seo.intro_paragraph: Start from Yogyakarta, Java's cultural capital, where Borobudur and Prambanan raise their stupas and spires just 40 km apart — two UNESCO masterpieces built by rival dynasties in the 9th century. Cross the active volcanoes of Bromo and Kawah Ijen before catching the ferry to Bali, and finish with Sidemen's rice terraces, Munduk's waterfalls and the temple of Uluwatu suspended above the Indian Ocean.
seo.seo_hook: Borobudur at sunrise, Bromo in the clouds, Kawah Ijen's blue fire, Jatiluwih rice terraces — Indonesia at its most powerful.
featured_list: Yogyakarta: Borobudur, the world's largest Buddhist temple, with 2,670 bas-relief panels
Bromo: sunrise over the Tengger caldera with its 4 volcanoes in a row
Kawah Ijen: blue fire visible only in darkness, acid lake at pH 0.2
Bali: UNESCO Jatiluwih rice terraces, Besakih Mother Temple and Kecak dance at Uluwatu

places_taglines.ID::yogyakarta: Java's cultural capital, birthplace of the Borobudur and Prambanan temples
places_taglines.ID::cemoro-lawang: Village perched on the caldera rim, facing Mount Bromo
places_taglines.ID::licin: Mountain village at the foot of Kawah Ijen, refuge of the sulphur porters
places_taglines.ID::ubud: Bali's cultural heart — traditional dance and terraced rice paddies
places_taglines.ID::sidemen: Authentic eastern Bali valley in the shadow of Mount Agung
places_taglines.ID::munduk: Northern Bali mountain village between waterfalls and volcanic lakes
places_taglines.ID::seminyak: Bali chic and surf, between sea temples and beach clubs
places_taglines.ID::uluwatu: Sacred temple perched 70 metres on the cliff, Kecak dance at sunset

highlights: Borobudur: built between 750 and 850, the world's foremost Buddhist monument with 2,670 bas-reliefs — sunrise recommended before 7 am
Mount Bromo: active volcano at 2,329 m inside a 10-km caldera, with views of Semeru (3,676 m) erupting every 15 minutes
Kawah Ijen: blue fire visible only at night, acid lake at pH 0.2 — departure at 2 am from Paltuding, gas mask essential
Bali: Banyuwangi–Gilimanuk ferry (45 min, hourly departures), then UNESCO Jatiluwih rice terraces, Besakih temple and Kecak dance at Uluwatu

itineraries.title: Borneo–Java–Bali Expedition: Orangutans, Temples and Volcanoes
itineraries.subtitle: 14 days from Kalimantan to Bali — from the tropical rainforests of Borneo to the Hindu temples of Java and the sacred cliffs of the Island of the Gods
itineraries.notes: This itinerary combines off-the-beaten-track destinations (East Kalimantan) with classic highlights (Bali, Bromo). Good physical fitness is recommended for the jungle treks at Kutai and the night at Cemoro Lawang (altitude 2,217 m, very cold). Kutai National Park requires a mandatory local guide; book in advance through a Sangata-based operator. Banyuwangi–Gilimanuk ferry: daily crossings of about 45 min.

visits.text: Balikpapan is your gateway to East Kalimantan — this port city built into the hillsides around the bay was once a simple Bugis fishing village before becoming a major oil hub. Head down to the waterfront to watch traditional boats and the stilt neighbourhoods that line the bay.

Klandasan Market, in the lower part of town, is the liveliest spot to feel the pulse of Balikpapan. You'll find spices, fresh fish, and Borneo fruits like rambutan and mangosteen. Ideally arrive in the late afternoon when activity picks up again after the midday heat.

Pantai Kemala, the beach closest to the city centre, offers a fine view of the bay and the forested hills at sunset. It's not a postcard beach, but it's where Balikpapan residents gather in the evening — you'll see an ordinary and authentic Indonesia, far from the tourist trail.

activities.text: After an international flight, spend your first evening recovering from jet lag. The streets around your hotel are lined with warungs where you can sample the local fare: mie goreng, nasi goreng, or Balikpapan-style grilled fish. An early night is recommended — the coming days in the forest start at dawn.

visits.text: The Samboja Lestari reserve lies about 45 km from Balikpapan — allow 1.5 hours by 4×4. This rehabilitation centre, run by the Borneo Orangutan Survival Foundation (BOSF), takes in orangutans and sun bears rescued from deforested areas. Since its founding in 2001, over one million trees representing 1,000 species have been replanted across 2,000 hectares. Visits run at 8 am and 1 pm, each lasting about 4 hours: don't miss feeding time on the floating islands designed for orangutans that cannot be released into the wild.

Bukit Bangkirai, about 1.5 hours' drive from Samboja, is home to a 1,500-hectare protected tropical forest dominated by bangkirai — hardwood trees standing 40 to 50 m tall, some 150 years old and up to 2.3 m in diameter. The canopy bridge connecting five bangkirai trees hangs at a height of 30 to 40 m and stretches 64 m: the view over Borneo's emerald jungle from the top is worth every step. Arrive early — gibbons, hornbills and red langurs are most active in the morning.

activities.text: In the late afternoon, board a motorised canoe on the Sungai Hitam (Black River) to cruise along the mangroves of Balikpapan Bay. This is your best chance of spotting proboscis monkeys in their natural habitat. These Borneo-endemic primates with their prominent noses are most visible at sunset, when they return to the riverside trees for the night.

visits.text: The drive from Balikpapan to Sangata takes roughly 6 to 7 hours — you'll cross the equator (marked by a monument), pass through palm-oil plantations and Dayak hamlets. Sangata is the access town for Kutai National Park, one of the last tracts of primary rainforest in East Kalimantan, first declared a reserve in 1936 by the Sultan of Kutai. Spanning 200,000 hectares, the park is home to around 2,000 wild orangutans (as counted in 2010), along with 90 mammal species and 300 bird species.

In the evening in Sangata, walk down to the banks of the Sangatta River for a short sunset stroll. The estuaries and riverbanks are transit zones for freshwater crocodiles and macaques. Sangata's residents live in daily coexistence with the forest — the local markets sell fruit and vegetables grown on the jungle's edge.

activities.text: Evening briefing with your local guide to prepare the next day's excursion into Kutai National Park. The park guides are rangers or wildlife specialists who know the sectors frequented by orangutans. Pack long sleeves, insect repellent and solid walking shoes — the forest is dense and humid.

map_keywords: Prevab Research Station Kutai National Park
Sangkima Flora Preservation Kutai National Park

visits.text: Very early morning departure by motorised canoe from Sangata's port — allow 20 minutes of navigation to reach the park's research station. The Prevab station, one of Indonesia's oldest orangutan research centres, is the starting point for guided forest treks with the rangers. The forest is lowland dipterocarp tropical — trees soaring 30 to 40 m, vines, and an incredible acoustic backdrop of bird calls and gibbon song. The orangutans living here are completely wild, with no guarantee of a sighting — that's precisely what makes the experience so rare.

The Sangkima area, on the park's eastern side, is a flora reserve containing one of Borneo's oldest trees — an ulin (ironwood), an endemic species that is virtually rot-proof. The mangrove forest at the mouth of the Sangatta River delta is a zone for canoe excursions to spot proboscis monkeys, false gharial crocodiles and around a hundred bird species, including 8 species of hornbill.

Sangata's market and the riverbanks in the evening offer a window into the daily life of a frontier town caught between the industrial world (Pertamina oil operations) and the forest. Local operators run sunset canoe trips on the Sangatta River to observe false gharial crocodiles and giant kingfishers — a transition between jungle and coast that sums up East Kalimantan nicely.

activities.text: Guided forest trek in Kutai Park in search of wild orangutans. The best sightings happen early in the morning (around 7 am) and late afternoon (around 3 pm), when orangutans build their nests in the trees. If you still have energy in the evening, a short night walk with a headlamp lets you look for tarsiers — tiny nocturnal primates with enormous eyes, endemic to Borneo.

visits.text: The flight from Balikpapan to Jakarta takes about 2 hours. If your connection allows, use a few hours to explore Kota Tua, Jakarta's Dutch colonial quarter — the 17th-century VOC warehouses have been converted into cafés and cultural spaces, and the grand Fatahillah Square is lined with baroque-fronted buildings. The Wayang Museum, dedicated to Javanese puppetry, is just steps away.

The Museum Nasional Indonesia, opposite the Monas, holds one of the largest collections of archaeological artefacts in Southeast Asia — Hindu statues from Java, Dayak masks, ikat textiles from every island in the archipelago. It's a solid intellectual introduction to the cultures you'll be passing through. Allow at least 1.5 hours.

Glodok, Jakarta's Chinatown just steps from Kota Tua, is one of the oldest Chinese quarters in Southeast Asia — Hakka traders settled here as early as the 17th century under the VOC. The alleyways overflow with herbalists, incense-filled Buddhist temples and small Peranakan restaurants. An extra layer of immersion in an already kaleidoscopic city.

activities.text: Jakarta is a transit stop on this itinerary — don't try to see everything. In the evening, the Menteng neighbourhood or the streets around Jl Sabang are packed with warungs and restaurants where you can dine cheaply alongside Jakartans. Jakarta is also notorious for its monstrous traffic jams: if you take a taxi, allow double the estimated time in the evening.

visits.text: Borobudur lies about 40 km northwest of Yogyakarta — leave early in the morning to beat the heat and the tour buses that arrive after 9 am. This Mahayana Buddhist temple, built between 778 and 850 AD under the Sailendra dynasty, is the largest Buddhist monument in the world: 2 million stones assembled without mortar to form a 33.5-m-high pyramid on a 120-m-wide base. Its 2,672 carved relief panels and 504 Buddha statues depict the path to nirvana across three symbolic levels. The temple was buried under volcanic ash around the year 1000 and rediscovered in 1814 by Raffles. It was listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1991 after a 10-year restoration carried out with the organisation's support.

The Kraton of Yogyakarta, founded in 1755 by Sultan Hamengkubuwono I after the Treaty of Giyanti split the Mataram kingdom into two sultanates, is the only still-inhabited royal palace in Indonesia.
