The 184-hectare complex is laid out according to Javanese cosmological principles — the Kraton, the commemorative column and Mount Merapi align perfectly on a north–south axis. Inside: collections of royal carriages, gamelan instruments, kris daggers and wayang puppets. Javanese dance performances are held in the open pavilions on certain mornings — check at the entrance.

Taman Sari, the water castle built in 1758 by Hamengkubuwono I west of the Kraton, was the royal pleasure garden. The complex originally included bathing pools, underground passages and a maze of corridors used, according to tradition, for the sultan's meditations. The central courtyard with its filled pools has been restored — the surrounding ruins carry a romantic charm that the children of Hamengkubuwono must still haunt.

activities.text: Jalan Malioboro, the philosophical axis of Yogyakarta linking the Tugu Golong Giling to the Kraton, is the city's busiest commercial artery. It's lined with batik shops, street vendors and souvenir sellers. If you're after authentic batik, head for the workshops tucked into the side streets behind the main road — the prices are more honest. In the evening, street musicians play gamelan and keroncong (a genre with Portuguese influences).

visits.text: Prambanan, 17 km northeast of Yogyakarta on the road to Solo, is the largest Hindu temple complex in Indonesia. Built in the 9th century by the Sanjaya dynasty of the Mataram kingdom, the site is UNESCO-listed. The central temple dedicated to Shiva rises to 47 m — its walls are covered with bas-reliefs narrating the Ramayana, some episodes of which are re-enacted every evening in the open-air amphitheatre at sunset (the Ramayana Ballet, a magnificent show with the illuminated temple as a backdrop). If you have time, the Sewu and Ratu Boko temples (a ruined palatial site on a hilltop with sweeping views of Merapi and Prambanan) are well worth the detour.

Mount Merapi (2,930 m) is the most active volcano in Indonesia — its last major eruption in 2010 claimed 353 lives. Jeeps run excursions up the northern slopes from the village of Kaliurang. You'll see fossilised lava flows and houses partly buried by the pyroclastic surge. The Lava Museum, built at the foot of the volcano, preserves testimony from the eruption and a car twisted by the heat, left in place as a memorial.

activities.text: Jeep excursion on the slopes of Merapi departing from Kaliurang — local operators offer 2-to-3-hour circuits at dawn or in the evening. The view of the caldera from the slopes is striking, and the landscape of ash and regrowing vegetation tells the story of the eruptions' violence and the resilience of the villages that rebuild in the volcano's shadow better than any text could.

The Ramayana Ballet at sunset on Prambanan's outdoor stage is an experience not to be missed if you're there on a Tuesday, Thursday or Saturday — dancers in golden costumes bring the Hindu epic to life with the illuminated temple as a backdrop. Book in advance; seats facing the stage sell out quickly.

practical_info.booking: Advance booking recommended

visits.text: The Sonobudoyo Museum, steps from the Kraton, is the best place to understand Javanese arts and history before leaving Yogyakarta: wayang kulit (shadow puppets), kris (ritual daggers, each believed to carry its own character depending on how it was forged), batik textiles and archaeological collections from Central Java. In the evening, wayang kulit performances are held in the museum courtyard.

Fort Vredeburg, built by the Dutch in 1760 at the request of Sultan Hamengkubuwono I to keep watch over the Kraton, has been turned into a museum of the Indonesian independence struggle. The dioramas cover the Japanese occupation, the 1945 declaration of independence and the revolutionary period when Yogyakarta briefly served as the capital of the Republic. The irony: the fort designed to control the sultan became the memorial to Javanese resistance against the colonisers.

Beringharjo Market, at the entrance to Malioboro, is one of the largest traditional markets on Java. The upper floors overflow with batik by the metre, kebayas and traditional fabrics — this is where Yogyakartans shop for ceremonial cloth. Bring someone who speaks Bahasa to negotiate, or accept that listed prices are often double the real price.

activities.text: Yogyakarta is the world capital of batik — the hot-wax resist technique applied with a canting (a small copper stylus) has been inscribed on UNESCO's Intangible Cultural Heritage list since 2009. Numerous workshops around Taman Sari offer half-day batik classes where you can create your own piece. A far more original souvenir than a t-shirt.

practical_info.booking: Booking recommended

map_keywords: Pura Luhur Poten Hindu Temple Bromo

visits.text: The drive from Yogyakarta to Cemoro Lawang takes about 5 hours via Probolinggo. Arrive before sunset for a first glimpse of the Tengger caldera from the village rim. Cemoro Lawang (2,217 m) sits perched on the edge of this caldera — below stretches the Sea of Sand (Segara Wedi), an 11-km-long ash plain protected as a nature reserve since 1919. Mount Bromo (2,329 m) smokes continuously in the middle of the caldera — its name comes from the Javanese pronunciation of Brahma, the Hindu god of creation.

Pura Luhur Poten, at the foot of Bromo on the Sea of Sand, is the Hindu temple of the Tenggerese — one of the last Hindu peoples on Java, believed to be descendants of a princess from the Majapahit kingdom. Every year between June and July, they celebrate the Yadnya Kasada festival by throwing offerings into the Bromo crater from the caldera rim. If you happen upon a ceremony, show respect.

activities.text: Wake-up at 3 am to climb to the Penanjakan viewpoint (2,770 m) — the highest point on the caldera's northern rim — before dawn. 4×4 jeeps shuttle from the village, but if you're a strong walker with a headlamp, the hike takes about 1 h 45 min from Cemoro Lawang via the Seruni viewpoint (about 1 h) to the summit. Sunrise falls between 5 and 5:30 am depending on the month. On a clear day, you'll see Bromo smoking inside the caldera with Mount Semeru (3,676 m, Java's tallest volcano) in the background — a scene found nowhere else on earth. Bring a warm jacket: temperatures drop to 5–10 °C at this altitude before dawn.

practical_info.duration: 3 h return on foot, 1 h 30 min by jeep

activities.text: After sunrise, descend into the Sea of Sand and walk up to the rim of Bromo's crater (250 steps). The crater floor smokes continuously — the sulphur smell is noticeable but bearable when there's wind. Avoid days with no wind or volcanic alerts. The climb from the Sea of Sand car park takes about 45 minutes on foot.

visits.text: From Cemoro Lawang, the drive to Banyuwangi (Java's eastern port) takes about 3–4 hours. The Banyuwangi–Gilimanuk ferry crosses the Bali Strait — less than a kilometre wide at this point — in roughly 45 minutes. Ferries run all day with frequent departures. Once in Gilimanuk, allow another 2.5 hours to Ubud via the north coast or the island's interior.

The Sacred Monkey Forest Sanctuary in Ubud spans 12 hectares right in the heart of town. It's home to over 700 long-tailed macaques and three ancient temples dating from the 14th century: Pura Dalem Agung, Pura Beji and Pura Prajapati, all still used for ceremonies. The monkeys are sacred to the locals — they're believed to protect the temple from negative influences. Keep a firm grip on your glasses and camera: these macaques are light-fingered.

If you take the north coast road from Gilimanuk, make a stop at Pura Pulaki — a cliff temple suspended between sea and mountain, known for its colony of semi-wild macaques occupying the stairways. Continuing east, Lovina is a quiet beach town where wild dolphins can be spotted by boat early in the morning. The north coast road is noticeably more scenic and far less busy than the central route.

activities.text: Arrival in Ubud after a long transit day: treat yourself to a Balinese massage on your first evening. The local technique combines deep kneading, frangipani oil and acupressure — an hour is enough to recover from a night on Mount Bromo. Ubud's spas are plentiful and considerably cheaper than those in the south of Bali.

visits.text: The Tegalalang Rice Terraces, 8 km north of Ubud, are the symbol of the subak irrigation technique — a system of hydraulic cooperatives between Balinese villages inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2012. The terraces cascade in tiers down the valley, forming a landscape of green waterfalls that photographers chase in the golden morning or late-afternoon light. Arrive before 8 am to beat the tour buses.

Tirta Empul, 15 km from Ubud towards Tampaksiring, is a purification temple built in 962 AD around a sacred spring whose water, according to legend, was created by the god Indra to resurrect his soldiers. You can watch — or take part, if you respect the dress code — in the ritual baths in the purification pools. This is a living practice, not a performance: Balinese families come here to purify themselves for deeply personal spiritual reasons.

Goa Gajah (Elephant Cave) dates from the 11th century — a life-size monstrous mouth sculpted into the rock marks the entrance to the cave sanctuary. Inside, the chamber houses statues of Ganesh and Shiva lingams. In front of the entrance, two carved fountains were used for ritual ablutions. The site is surprisingly little-visited despite its proximity to Ubud.

The Campuhan Ridge Walk, a 15-minute stroll on foot from central Ubud, follows a green ridge between two rivers — the Sungai Wos to the east and the Sungai Cebok to the west. The paved path passes rice paddies, small temples and open views over the wooded hills. Ideal at dawn or late afternoon. Allow about 1.5 hours for the return walk.

activities.text: Ubud is the centre of Balinese art — ever since the 1930s, when European artists such as Walter Spies and Rudolf Bonnet settled here and stimulated local creativity. The craft market and the Museum Puri Lukisan (Balinese painting) or the Agung Rai Museum of Art show how that dialogue transformed the Balinese pictorial style. In the evening, Legong, Barong or Kecak performances are staged at several venues in the village — the most beautiful setting remains the Puri Saren (Ubud Royal Palace).

visits.text: Seminyak, on Bali's southwest coast, is the island's upscale neighbourhood — Balinese designer boutiques, fine-dining restaurants and beach clubs line the lanes of Jl Kayu Aya and Jl Petitenget. Seminyak's beach can have rough waters (strong rip currents — check the flags before going in), but it's perfect for watching the sunset from a bean bag with a cocktail in hand.

Tanah Lot, 20 km north of Seminyak, is one of Bali's seven sea temples — a rocky platform jutting out of the ocean with a 16th-century temple perched on top. According to Balinese tradition, each coastal temple should be visible from the next to form a chain of protection. The sunset light on the temple is photographed by millions of visitors — arrive an hour before sunset to find a good angle before the crowds.

activities.text: Canggu, the bohemian neighbourhood 5 km north of Seminyak, has become the playground of surfers, creatives and digital nomads. Echo Beach and Batu Bolong offer consistent waves suited to all levels — local operators run surf lessons. In Canggu's lanes, street-art murals, trendy cafés and vintage shops create an atmosphere radically different from the rest of Bali.

Sunset from Petitenget or Double Six beach, drink in hand at one of the beach clubs lining the Seminyak coast. The evening light is particularly beautiful here because the coast faces due west over the Indian Ocean. It's the daily ritual for Balinese residents and foreign expats alike — sundowners with a view of the horizon.

visits.text: Dedicate this day to a relaxed exploration of Seminyak and Canggu. The Balinese designer workshops around Jl Kayu Aya sell locally made clothing, ceramics and jewellery — purchases far more interesting than the mass-produced souvenirs of Kuta. Seminyak's morning market is frequented by the cooks of the neighbourhood's villas and restaurants.

The artisan villages north of Seminyak are worth a visit if you have a driver for the day: Celuk specialises in silver jewellery, Mas in wood carving, Batuan in traditional painting. You can watch the artisans at work and buy directly from their workshops — prices bear no resemblance to those in the town-centre shops.

activities.text: A surf session at Echo Beach or Batu Bolong in Canggu, if you haven't tried it yet. The waves on this coast are ideal for beginners — sandy bottom, consistent swell, patient local instructors. Batu Bolong Beach is also where the local surf community gathers in the late afternoon.

Massage and treatments at one of Seminyak's many spas — the town has dozens, from neighbourhood salons to high-end establishments. The traditional Balinese massage, the lulur (a turmeric and rice scrub) and the boreh (a warming spice wrap) are the local specialities to try before catching your flight home.

visits.text: Pura Luhur Uluwatu is perched 70 m above the Indian Ocean on a limestone cliff on the Bukit peninsula. The temple dates back to the 9th century — an inscription from 886 AD confirms it — and was expanded in the 11th century by the sage Mpu Kuturan, then embellished in the 15th century by Danghyang Nirartha, a Brahmin priest who according to tradition achieved moksha (spiritual liberation) here — his cloak is said to have turned to stone, forming the very foundation of the temple. It is one of the six directional temples (Sad Kahyangan) believed to protect Bali from evil forces coming from the sea. Be warned: the temple's macaques are expert at snatching sunglasses, phones and open bags.

Padang Padang Beach, reached by a staircase carved into the cliff below Uluwatu, is a cove of turquoise water framed by rocks. It's one of Bali's best surf spots for experienced surfers (reef break — not for beginners). For non-surfers, swimming is pleasant at low tide. The beach was made world-famous by the film Eat Pray Love.

The Kecak dance at Uluwatu is performed every evening at 6 pm in the temple's open-air amphitheatre, with the ocean and the sunset as a backdrop. Around 75 male dancers form a circle chanting the rhythmic 'cak-cak-cak' chorus without any musical instruments — a technique born in the 1930s when Balinese dancer Wayan Limbak and German painter Walter Spies adapted the Sanghyang trance ritual into a theatrical narrative based on the Ramayana. The scene with the illuminated cliffs in the background, the fire and the chant building to a crescendo is one of the most powerful cultural experiences in Bali. Arrive 30 minutes before the show for a good spot. Ngurah Rai Airport in Denpasar is a 30-minute drive from Uluwatu.

activities.text: Bingin Beach, reached via a steep 10-minute path from the main road, is a small white-sand beach at the foot of Uluwatu's cliffs. The water is clear and the beach rarely crowded on weekdays. Bamboo warungs on the sand serve grilled fish and fresh juices — the perfect lunch to round off this Indonesian expedition.

seo.h1_title: Borneo–Java–Bali Expedition: Orangutans, Temples and Volcanoes
seo.intro_paragraph: Set out to meet the last wild orangutans of Borneo in the primary rainforests of Kutai National Park, then cross to Java where Borobudur — the largest Buddhist temple on earth, built in 778 AD — towers over the Kedu Plain. Walk across the Bromo Sea of Sand at dawn, then let Bali wrap you up between Ubud's rice terraces and the sacred cliffs of Uluwatu.
seo.seo_hook: Wild orangutans at Kutai, UNESCO temples on Java, sunrise over Mount Bromo, Kecak dance facing the ocean at Uluwatu.
featured_list: Samboja Lestari & Kutai: orangutan rehabilitation and wildlife observation in Kalimantan's primary rainforest
Borobudur & Prambanan: the two largest Buddhist and Hindu monuments in the world, 40 km apart
Bromo: sunrise over the Tengger caldera from 2,770 m, with a smoking volcano below
Uluwatu: 15th-century temple on a 70-m cliff, sunset Kecak dance facing the Indian Ocean

Kutai National Park: 200,000 hectares of primary rainforest with around 2,000 wild orangutans recorded

itineraries.title: Kalimantan: Orangutans, Primary Rainforests and the Derawan Islands in 15 Days
itineraries.subtitle: From Balikpapan to Putussibau — a complete crossing of Indonesian Borneo, with orangutan sanctuaries, diving in the Derawan archipelago, the Banjarmasin floating market and Dayak longhouses.
itineraries.notes: Itinerary requiring several domestic flights: Balikpapan→Berau (or via Samarinda), Berau→Tarakan, Tarakan→Banjarmasin, Pelaihari→Pontianak, Pontianak→Putussibau. Transfers to the Derawan Islands are by speedboat from Tanjung Batu (Berau port). The visit to Kakaban's jellyfish lake may be subject to temporary closures — check before departure. Dry season recommended (April–October) for diving and volcanoes.

visits.text: Balikpapan, nicknamed 'the oil city' of Borneo, was born on 10 February 1897 when the Dutch company Mijnbouw Maatschappij struck its first wells. Today it is a modern port city built into the hillsides around a vast bay, and the main entry point for East Kalimantan. From the airport, head straight for the waterfront to gauge the scale of the bay — tankers, phinisi sailing boats and fishermen's canoes share the anchorage. The contrast between the glass towers of the oil district and the stilt houses along the shore tells the story of the city in a single glance.

The Margomulyo Mangrove Forest lines the seafront north of Balikpapan. You walk along wooden boardwalks built through thick mangrove stands, where proboscis monkeys and long-tailed macaques are regularly spotted. The trail takes about an hour and is mostly flat. It's a fine first contact with Borneo's coastal ecosystems before heading inland.

activities.text: Kemala Beach, in the southwest of the city, is Balikpapan's most accessible beach. Lively bars line the sand and locals gather at sunset — the view across the bay as the sky turns orange is a fitting welcome to Kalimantan. Not a postcard beach, but a genuine slice of everyday Indonesian life.

The Kebun Sayur craft market is a surprise for anyone expecting a vegetable market — its name means 'vegetable garden' but the stalls overflow with Dayak souvenirs, Kalimantan pearls, local fabric and carved ironwood. It's the best place in Balikpapan to pick up handicrafts before heading into the interior.

map_keywords: Bukit Bangkirai canopy bridge East Kalimantan

visits.text: Samboja Lestari is one of the world's best-known orangutan sanctuaries, run by the Borneo Orangutan Survival Foundation (BOSF). Located 38 km from Balikpapan (about 1.5 hours by car), the reserve covers 1,800 hectares of forest that has been replanted tree by tree since 2001 — over a million trees representing 1,000 species. Around 120 orangutans and 70 sun bears rescued from deforested areas are being rehabilitated here. Visits take place at 8 am and 1 pm, each lasting about 4 hours. Don't miss feeding time on the floating islands designed for orangutans that cannot be released into the wild — the animals' behaviour during feeding is extraordinary.

About 1.5 hours' drive from Samboja, the Bukit Bangkirai hills shelter a 1,500-hectare dipterocarp forest where bangkirai hardwood trees tower 40 to 50 m, some 150 years old and up to 2.3 m in diameter. The canopy bridge, stretching 64 m at a height of 30 to 40 m and connecting five bangkirai trees, offers vertiginous views over Borneo's emerald jungle. Arrive early — gibbons, hornbills and red langurs are most active in the morning.

activities.text: The protected forest of the Sungai Wain river, 40 km from Balikpapan, is one of the last fragments of primary lowland forest in the region. A 3-hour guided trek through the dense canopy offers chances to spot orangutans, sun bears, hornbills and dozens of butterfly species. The humidity is intense — bring plenty of water and wear long sleeves.

map_keywords: Kutai National Park Prevab orangutan station

visits.text: Kutai National Park is one of Indonesia's oldest: declared a nature reserve in 1934 by the Sultan of Kutai, it covers 192,709 hectares of primary lowland rainforest. The Prevab research station, one of the country's oldest for orangutan studies, is the starting point for guided treks with rangers. The forest is classic lowland dipterocarp — trees soaring 30 to 40 m, vines, and an incredible soundscape of bird calls and gibbon song. The orangutans here are completely wild, with no guarantee of a sighting — which is precisely what makes the experience so valuable. Over 300 bird species have been recorded in the park, including 7 species of hornbill.

The Sangkima forest walk, accessible from Bontang in about an hour, is the third gateway into Kutai Park. Less demanding than the Prevab sector, it follows boardwalks through mangroves and lowland forest housing one of Borneo's oldest trees — an ulin (ironwood), a virtually rot-proof endemic species. Proboscis monkeys, macaques and hornbills are commonly spotted along the boardwalk.

activities.text: Teluk Kaba bay, between Bontang and Sangatta, is reached in 45 minutes by speedboat from Bontang port. It's one of the park's best-kept secrets: a sandy cove backed by primary forest where green turtles come to nest. The snorkelling offshore reveals healthy coral and reef fish. Bring your own mask — facilities are minimal.

visits.text: The Islamic Center Samarinda mosque (Baitul Muttaqien) is the second-largest mosque in Southeast Asia after Jakarta's Istiqlal. Its seven golden minarets, dome spanning 35 m and 15,000-person capacity dominate the Samarinda skyline. The building, completed in 2018 after 20 years of construction, blends Kutai Malay, Ottoman and Andalusian architectural influences. Non-Muslims can visit outside prayer times — the panoramic terrace offers a sweeping view of the Mahakam.

The Mahakam River is Borneo's third-longest watercourse at 980 km, from its sources in the central highlands down to the Makassar Strait. In Samarinda, the river is about 500 m wide and teeming with activity: cargo barges loaded with coal, fishermen casting nets, and klotok wooden boats that serve as the local bus network. Rent a klotok from the main wharf and head upstream for a few hours — the further you go, the wilder the riverbanks become.

The Pesut Mahakam is the freshwater Irrawaddy dolphin (Orcaella brevirostris) — the official emblem of East Kalimantan province. Fewer than 80 individuals remain in the Mahakam, making it one of the most critically endangered freshwater cetacean populations on earth. The best viewing zone is between Muara Kaman and Muara Muntai, about 3 hours upstream from Samarinda. Sightings are not guaranteed but the river journey itself through flooded forests and stilt villages is reward enough.

The Pasar Pagi neighbourhood is the historic trading heart of Samarinda, with the Great Mosque Darussalam built in 1882 by Sultan Aji Muhammad Sulaiman and the old Chinese trading quarter nearby. The streets are noisy, narrow and lined with food stalls — a genuine slice of Mahakam-river-town life without any tourist gloss.

activities.text: The Mahakam Bridge promenade in Samarinda is lit in rainbow colours at night. Floating cafés moored alongside the bridge serve coffee and fried snacks while you watch the cargo barges drift past. It's the most popular evening hangout in town.

Local operators run canoe excursions on Mahakam tributaries to visit Dayak villages upstream of Samarinda. The houses are more modest than the longhouses of upper Kalimantan, but the hospitality is genuine — expect offers of tuak (palm wine) and stories about the river. Allow a full half-day.

map_keywords: Tanjung Batu port Berau speedboat Derawan

visits.text: Berau is the provincial town that serves as the gateway to the Derawan archipelago — one of the world's top marine biodiversity hotspots, ranked second globally for coral diversity with over 500 species. The town itself is compact and functional. From Berau, speedboats leave from Tanjung Batu port (about 2 hours from the town centre) for the 1-to-2-hour crossing to the islands.

Tanjung Redeb, Berau's capital, is split by the confluence of two rivers — the Kelay and the Segah — which merge to form the Berau River. The Gunung Tabur Palace, built in 1872 and now a museum, was the seat of the Gunung Tabur sultanate. Inside you'll find royal weapons, traditional costumes and historical photographs. The views from the palace over the river junction are the best in town.

Not far away on the Kelay River, the Sambaliung Palace is the other relic of the two rival sultanates created in the early 18th century from the original Berau sultanate. Smaller and less restored than Gunung Tabur, it has a quieter charm and fewer visitors.

If you arrive early in Berau, make the detour to Labuan Cermin lake in the Biduk-Biduk sub-district, about 6 hours south of Berau. This extraordinary two-layer lake has freshwater on the surface and saltwater on the bottom, creating such clarity that the rock formations below are visible as if through glass. It's a long drive for a single stop but absolutely worth it if your schedule allows.

activities.text: The evening in Berau is the time to prepare for the coming days in the archipelago: book your diving and snorkelling trips, check the latest conditions at Kakaban's jellyfish lake (temporary closures are possible) and stock up on sunscreen and cash — ATMs and shops are scarce on the islands.

map_keywords: Pulau Derawan dive site blue-ringed octopus

visits.text: Derawan Island is the main hub of the eponymous archipelago — 31 islands and reefs spread over 160 km of coastline. Drop your bags at your guesthouse and head straight to the jetty: green turtles swim just metres from the wooden piers, surfacing to breathe every few minutes. The island is small enough to walk around in an hour. The waters immediately offshore teem with life — clownfish, nudibranchs, seahorses and juvenile reef sharks are visible in waist-deep water.

Pulau Gusung is a white sandbar submerged at high tide and exposed at low tide, 10–15 minutes by boat from Derawan. At its peak, it's a ribbon of pure white sand surrounded by turquoise water with no vegetation at all — one of the most photogenic spots in the archipelago. Bring sun protection — there is zero shade.

Sangalaki Island, a 2-hour speedboat ride from Derawan, is one of the world's best stations for swimming with manta rays. The mantas gather here year-round to feed on plankton concentrations. The island is also a major green-turtle nesting site — between June and September, up to 50 turtles come ashore each night to lay eggs.

activities.text: Night diving off Derawan is an experience apart: turtles sleep on the seabed, octopuses come alive, and phosphorescent plankton light up the water column with every fin stroke. Several dive operators on the island offer guided night dives — book in the morning for an evening slot.

practical_info.booking: Booking recommended on the morning of

map_keywords: Maratua atoll Big Fish Country dive

visits.text: Kakaban's lake is one of the strangest natural curiosities on the planet. Kakaban — whose name means 'to embrace' in the local language — is a raised coral atoll whose interior lagoon became cut off from the sea roughly 2 million years ago. Trapped inside, four species of jellyfish evolved to lose their stinging cells entirely (they had no predators to defend against). Today you can swim among millions of translucent, pulsating, completely harmless jellyfish in warm, slightly brackish water. The sensation of being surrounded by these gentle creatures in an enclosed jungle lake is unlike anything else.

Maratua is the largest atoll in the Derawan archipelago, perched at the edge of the continental shelf where the seabed plunges into the deep Sulawesi Sea. The diving here is known as Big Fish Country: barracuda walls, grey reef sharks, eagle rays, whale sharks (seasonal) and giant trevally patrol the outer reef. The coral gardens on the inside of the atoll are equally spectacular, with pristine table corals and visibility regularly exceeding 30 m.

activities.text: In the late afternoon on Maratua, stroll through one of the island's Bajau villages and observe the stilt houses built over the reef flat. The Bajau — also known as 'sea nomads' — have lived on or above the water for centuries. Their freedivers are legendary, hunting fish at depths of 20 m or more on a single breath. The village sunset over the turquoise lagoon is a quiet, memorable end to the day.

map_keywords: Mangrove Conservation Area proboscis monkeys Tarakan

visits.text: Tarakan is an island-city in northern Kalimantan with a particularly intense wartime history: it was the first target of the Japanese invasion of the Dutch East Indies on 11 January 1942, seized for its oil reserves. The Allied recapture in May 1945 was one of the last major battles of the Pacific War. Today, remnants of bunkers and Japanese tunnels can be visited in the hills above the town. The small WWII memorial park near the airport recounts the sequence of events.

Right in central Tarakan, the Mangrove and Proboscis Monkey Conservation Reserve is a small green surprise: a boardwalk trail through dense mangrove that is home to a resident population of proboscis monkeys — the big-nosed primates endemic to Borneo. The best viewing times are early morning and late afternoon when they come to the trees nearest the boardwalk.

Amal Beach, 11 km from the town centre, is Tarakan's best seaside spot. Under the coconut palms, stalls sell fried fish and fresh coconut water while local families enjoy the weekend. The water is warm but murky — it's more a social beach than a snorkelling destination.

activities.text: Tarakan's Orchid Garden (Taman Anggrek) brings together dozens of wild orchid species from Borneo, including the famous Black Orchid (Coelogyne pandurata), a rare endemic species that has become the floral emblem of East Kalimantan. The garden is small but beautifully maintained and worth a 45-minute visit.

map_keywords: Lok Baintan Floating Market Banjarmasin sunrise

visits.text: Banjarmasin, the 'city of a thousand rivers' in South Kalimantan, sits just centimetres above sea level on a delta laced with canals, tributaries and tidal channels. Life here revolves around water: houses are built on rafts or stilts, transport runs by klotok boat, and the daily rhythms of trade, worship and socialising all happen on or beside the river. The Sabilal Muhtadin Grand Mosque, rising above the Martapura River, is the city's landmark — its green dome and minarets are visible from every bridge.

The Lok Baintan floating market is one of Indonesia's oldest living traditions: women in wide-brimmed hats paddle jukung canoes loaded with fruit, vegetables, cakes and spices, trading boat-to-boat in the middle of the Martapura River. The market has operated in some form since the Banjar sultanate in the 16th century. Arrive by klotok from the Banua Anyar wharf (about 45 minutes upstream) before 7 am to see the market at peak activity — by 9:30 it's winding down.

activities.text: A klotok cruise through Banjarmasin's canals reveals the city's daily life better than any walking tour: women washing clothes on the riverbank, children diving off jetties, floating mosques moored in side channels and vendors paddling from house to house. The network of waterways is vast — allow at least 2 hours to explore the inner canals beyond the main river.

visits.text: Martapura, 45 km from Banjarmasin, is the diamond and gemstone capital of Kalimantan. The Pasar Intan (diamond market) is a bustling hall where dealers display loose stones — diamonds, sapphires, amethysts and agates mined from the surrounding hills. Bargaining is expected. The nearby Cahaya Bumi Selamat mosque, built almost entirely from gemstone-encrusted materials, is an architectural curiosity worth a stop.

The Lambung Mangkurat Museum in Banjarbaru houses a remarkable ethnographic collection on Banjar culture — ceramics, Banjar wedding costumes, ancient weapons and documents tracing the region's history from the Hindu Negara Dipa kingdom to the Islamic Banjar sultanate and Dutch colonisation. Allow about an hour.

The Muara Kuin floating market, at the confluence of the Kuin and Barito rivers, is the oldest of Banjarmasin's floating markets. Smaller and more authentic than Lok Baintan, it caters almost exclusively to locals. Come before 7 am — the trading is over by dawn.

activities.text: The Radap Rahayu dance, a traditional Banjar dance depicting angels descending from heaven to bring wisdom, is occasionally performed at cultural centres or during ceremonies in the Banjarmasin area. Ask your accommodation if any performances are scheduled during your stay — it's one of the region's most graceful art forms.

map_keywords: Rimpi Hills Teletubbies Hills Pelaihari South Kalimantan
Pelaihari scenic hills sunset South Kalimantan
Tanah Laut regency South Kalimantan nature

visits.text: Pelaihari is the capital of Tanah Laut district ('sea of land'), 66 km from Banjarmasin. The area is known for its Rimpi Hills — undulating green hills that locals have nicknamed 'Teletubbies Hills' for their resemblance to the children's-show landscape. The hills are covered in low grass and scattered trees, offering panoramic views of the southern Kalimantan coast. The best time to visit is late afternoon when the light softens.

Bajuin Waterfall, 10 km from Pelaihari on the foothills of the Meratus range, comprises three successive drops in a forested gorge. The pools at the base are swimmable — the water is cool and surprisingly clear for a jungle stream. The trail takes about 3 hours round trip from the road.

The road from Banjarmasin to Pelaihari hugs the southern coast of Kalimantan, allowing stops at Sarang Tiung and Swarangan beaches — quiet stretches of sand where local fishing communities dry their catch in the sun. These are not resort beaches, but genuine working coastline with a character all their own.

activities.text: Sunset over the Rimpi Hills is Pelaihari's main draw: settle on the principal ridge and watch the green mounds turn golden as the light fades. The spot is popular with locals on weekends but quiet during the week. Bring a picnic — there are no vendors on the hill.

visits.text: Pontianak is the only provincial capital in the world to sit exactly on the equator — a distinction that has earned it the Tugu Khatulistiwa monument, first erected in 1928 by a Dutch expedition and since rebuilt as a full-scale landmark. A red line painted on the ground marks the 0° latitude. Twice a year, at the March and September equinoxes, vertical objects cast no shadow at noon — a phenomenon that draws small crowds of curious visitors.

The Keraton Kadriah, the royal palace of the Pontianak sultanate founded in 1771 by Syarif Abdurrahman Alkadrie, stands on the banks of the Kapuas River. The yellow wooden building houses a museum of royal regalia, weapons, textiles and photographs. The palace courtyard, shaded by old frangipani trees, is one of the most peaceful spots in a noisy city.

The Kapuas River is Indonesia's longest at 1,143 km, crossing the heart of West Kalimantan to the South China Sea. In Pontianak, the river is wide, muddy and busy — cargo boats, ferries and fishing canoes jostle for space. A short boat ride from the city centre gives you a feel for the sheer scale of this waterway, which serves as the main artery for communities deep in the Borneo interior.

activities.text: The Betang House — a reconstructed Dayak longhouse on Jalan M.T. Haryono in Pontianak — offers a preview of what awaits in Putussibau. The building demonstrates the communal architecture of the Dayak: a single roof sheltering an entire community, with shared corridors and individual family rooms. Guides explain the social structure and customs linked to longhouse living.

visits.text: The Flamboyan market, on the banks of the Kapuas in Pontianak, is the city's liveliest popular market, especially in the early morning. Fish, tropical fruit, spices and Dayak handicrafts fill the stalls. The riverside setting adds atmosphere — sampans unload produce straight onto the quayside.

The Tua Pek Kong pagoda and Pontianak's Chinatown bear witness to the Chinese presence in the city since the late 18th century. Hakka miners arrived in the Sambas and Monterado goldfields and gradually settled along the Kapuas. The temple, one of the oldest in West Kalimantan, is a colourful focal point in a neighbourhood of traditional Chinese shophouses.

activities.text: In the afternoon, head to the Kapuas riverbanks for a sunset sampan ride — the stilt houses, floating mosques and fishermen casting nets in the golden light make for a fitting farewell to Pontianak. If you have two extra days, an extension to Gunung Palung National Park (via a flight from Pontianak to Ketapang) offers wild orangutan encounters in pristine lowland forest — not included in this itinerary.

practical_info.note: Gunung Palung: 2-day extension recommended via Pontianak→Ketapang flight, not included in this itinerary

map_keywords: Betung Kerihun National Park entrance West Kalimantan

visits.text: Putussibau, 800 km upstream on the Kapuas from Pontianak, is the last town before the primary forest of the Borneo heartland. Accessible by a 1-hour flight from Pontianak, it sits at the confluence of several Kapuas tributaries and serves as the base for expeditions into Dayak Iban and Dayak Kayan territory. The town is quiet, dusty and utterly authentic — there's no tourist infrastructure beyond a handful of guesthouses and warungs.

The Sungai Utik longhouse, about 1.5 hours by road from Putussibau, is considered by many to be the most authentic inhabited Iban betang on Borneo. The structure, some 200 m long and housing over 300 people from 28 families, sits on the banks of the Utik River. The community has fiercely defended its surrounding forest against logging companies and was recognised with a UNDP Equator Prize in 2019. Visitors can sleep in the longhouse, share meals and witness daily rituals — advance arrangement through a Putussibau guide is essential.

activities.text: Betung Kerihun National Park, 2 hours from Putussibau by river along the Embaloh, covers 800,000 hectares of tropical rainforest — one of the largest intact forest blocks on Borneo. The park shelters wild orangutans, clouded leopards, sun bears and over 300 bird species. Multi-day treks with a mandatory guide follow river-and-trail routes connecting remote Dayak villages deep in the forest.

practical_info.booking: Guide mandatory

map_keywords: Danau Sentarum National Park wetlands West Kalimantan

visits.text: Danau Sentarum National Park, a 4-hour canoe ride downstream from Putussibau, is the largest wetland on the island of Borneo — 132,000 hectares of seasonal lakes, peat swamp forest and flooded grasslands. The park is home to over 240 freshwater fish species, many found nowhere else. Between October and January, the lakes swell to form an inland sea; in the dry season, they shrink to isolated pools teeming with fish. Dayak Iban fishing communities live inside the park in floating houses that rise and fall with the water level.

Return to Putussibau before the last flight to Pontianak. Before leaving, stroll along the Kapuas riverbanks upstream of the town — the wooden jetties, canoes and the forest pressing in from both banks give you one final image of the Borneo interior. Buy a bag of locally grown Kapuas coffee from the market as a souvenir.

activities.text: Putussibau has a small airport with daily connections to Pontianak (PSU→PNK, 1-hour flight). From Pontianak, connections to Jakarta, Surabaya or Kuching (Malaysia) are available. Check schedules well in advance — rotations are limited and can change. The journey ends here, at the edge of one of the last great rainforests on earth.

seo.h1_title: Kalimantan: Orangutans, Primary Rainforests and the Derawan Islands in 15 Days
seo.intro_paragraph: Start from Balikpapan, the oil-industry gateway to Indonesian Borneo, and head to Samboja Lestari where 120 orangutans are being rehabilitated in a forest replanted since 2001. Cross the 192,000-hectare Kutai National Park in search of wild orangutans, then fly east to the Derawan archipelago — one of the richest marine biodiversity hotspots on the planet, with 500 coral species, 5,000 nesting sea turtles a year and the stingless-jellyfish lake of Kakaban. Head south to Banjarmasin and its 16th-century Lok Baintan floating market, then west to Pontianak — the only provincial capital sitting exactly on the equator — before finishing in Putussibau at the edge of the Dayak heartland, where inhabited longhouses and 800,000 hectares of primary forest at Betung Kerihun await.
seo.seo_hook: Wild orangutans in Kutai, stingless jellyfish in Kakaban lake, diving the Big Fish Country of Maratua, Dayak longhouses — raw Kalimantan.
featured_list: Samboja Lestari: 1,800 hectares of restored forest, 120 orangutans and 70 sun bears under rehabilitation
Derawan: 500+ coral species, 5,000 turtles nesting per year, stingless jellyfish of Kakaban
Banjarmasin: Lok Baintan floating market since the 16th century, jukung canoes at sunrise
Putussibau: inhabited Dayak Iban longhouses, Betung Kerihun's 800,000 ha of primary forest
