highlights: Samboja Lestari: 1,800 hectares of restored forest 38 km from Balikpapan, 120 orangutans and 70 sun bears managed by BOSF — visit by reservation with feeding times at 8 am or 1 pm
Kutai: 192,709 hectares of primary forest, Prevab research station for wild orangutans, 300 bird species including 7 species of hornbill
Derawan-Kakaban: 500 coral species (2nd worldwide), 5,000 sea turtles per year, lake with 4 species of stingless jellyfish unique in the world — temporary closures possible
Putussibau: still-inhabited Dayak Iban longhouses, Betung Kerihun 800,000 ha, Danau Sentarum National Park 132,000 ha, 1-hour flight from Pontianak

itineraries.title: Northern Sumatra: Orangutans, Lake Toba and Tsunami Memorials in 14 Days
itineraries.subtitle: From Medan to the Karo volcanoes, from Lake Toba to the rice paddies of Harau, all the way to the memorials of Banda Aceh — 14 days in the heart of Sumatra's most authentic landscapes
itineraries.notes: Demanding circuit in terms of transport: long distances between stops, mountain roads. Plan overnight buses (Tuk-Tuk → Bukittinggi ~10h). D7→D8: Samosir → Bukittinggi via Parapat, approximately 8–10 hours' drive. D12→D13: Padang → Banda Aceh flight recommended (1h20), otherwise overnight bus ~14h. Banda Aceh is a city under Islamic law (sharia): modest clothing required, alcohol forbidden.

visits.text: Maimun Palace (Istana Maimun) commands attention as soon as you reach central Medan with its bright yellow facade — this colour is not a decorative whim but the symbol of Malay royalty. Built between 1888 and 1891 on the orders of the Sultan of Deli, Ma'mun Al Rashid Perkasa Alamsyah, it covers 2,772 m² and contains 30 rooms spread over two floors. Its architect, the Dutch captain Theodoor van Erp, blended Mughal, Spanish and Italian styles — look for the Moorish arches, wooden terraces and 'bamboo shoot' motifs on the roof symbolising fertility. The throne room is the only section open to the public: you can try on traditional Malay costumes there.

The Grand Mosque Al Mashun, just opposite Maimun Palace, was commissioned by the same sultan in August 1906 and completed in September 1909. Its plan is octagonal with wings facing the four cardinal points — a design not unlike Maghrebi mosques. What is less well known: its construction was partly funded by Tjong A Fie, Medan's wealthy Hakka merchant who was not Muslim. Enter before the midday prayer to admire the high ceilings and stained glass windows.

The Tjong A Fie Mansion, a ten-minute walk away in the Kesawan district, is one of the best-preserved merchant houses in Southeast Asia. Tjong A Fie arrived from China in 1875 aged 18 with a few silver coins sewn into his belt. By 1911, he was the recognised leader of Medan's Chinese community, owner of plantations, banks and the Medan–Belawan railway company. His house, completed around 1900, blends Sino-Portuguese, Victorian and Malay styles: 35 rooms, hand-painted floor tiles imported from Italy, and a ballroom on the upper floor. A guided tour is recommended — the man's story is more fascinating than the architecture.

activities.text: The Kesawan district, Medan's historic main street, is the best place for dinner on your first evening. Nineteenth-century Chinese trading houses line the streets with their arcaded facades. Look for a warung serving mie goreng or bihun Medan — Medan's cuisine is one of the most renowned in Sumatra, shaped by the Malays, Batak, Chinese and Tamil Indians present since colonial times.

visits.text: The Museum Negeri Sumatera Utara, housed in a building modelled on traditional Batak architecture, offers the best introduction to all the cultures you will encounter on this trip. The collections cover ancient Hindu civilisations of central Java, Batak Toba ulos textiles, gondang musical instruments, ritual kris daggers and archaeological pieces from the sultanate era. Allow 1h30 to see it properly.

The Sri Mariamman Hindu temple, steps away from Kesawan, is Medan's oldest Hindu temple — dating from 1884. Its Dravidian style, typical of Tamil Nadu, with a gateway tower (gopuram) adorned with colourful deities, contrasts with the neighbouring mosques. It bears witness to the Tamil workers recruited from the nineteenth century by Dutch companies for their plantations. Ceremonies are held on Friday mornings.

Medan's town hall (Pos Bloc), built in 1908 in an ornate neoclassical style, is one of the best-preserved colonial buildings in the city. Its clock tower was donated in 1913 by Tjong A Fie — him again. Stroll around Lapangan Merdeka, the large central park, to take in the Dutch colonial architecture that frames the city.

activities.text: Explore Pasar Petisah market, Medan's liveliest covered market, to stock up on provisions and sample local specialities before heading into the forest: fresh durian (if in season), bika ambon (rice flour and coconut cake) and sirup marquisa, the passion fruit syrup typical of the Karo Highlands. It is also the cheapest place to shop before Bukit Lawang.

map_keywords: Gunung Leuser National Park entrance Bukit Lawang

visits.text: Bukit Lawang is a village of 3,000 people perched on the Bohorok River, 78 km west of Medan. The road passes through rubber and oil palm plantations — this parade of monoculture contrasts starkly with the primary forest that begins here. As soon as you arrive, settle in on the riverside: bamboo cabins perched on the banks offer a direct view into the jungle and its gibbon concerts in the late afternoon. Book your trek this evening through a park-certified operator.

The Bat Cave, a twenty-minute walk from the village, houses a spectacular colony. Arrive around 5:30 pm for the fly-out: thousands of bats emerge in spiralling columns at dusk to hunt. It is the best foretaste of Gunung Leuser National Park's biodiversity — 7,927 km² of tropical forest sheltering the Sumatran orangutan, tiger, Sumatran rhinoceros and elephant.

activities.text: Walk through the village in the evening to choose your trekking guide. Bukit Lawang has been an eco-tourism hub since two Swiss nationals founded an orangutan rehabilitation centre here in 1973 — the centre has since closed and the animals released. Prioritise guides who are members of the national park's official local association: they follow safety rules (stay at least 10 metres away, never feed the animals). A one-day trek is enough to see semi-wild orangutans. Two days with an overnight in the forest offers a significantly deeper experience.

map_keywords: Gunung Leuser National Park orangutan trek

visits.text: Gunung Leuser National Park (TNGL), a UNESCO World Heritage Site along with Bukit Barisan Selatan and Kerinci Seblat parks under the 'Tropical Rainforest Heritage of Sumatra' designation, is one of only two remaining habitats of the Sumatran orangutan (Pongo abelii) on Earth. The park covers 7,927 km² — roughly 150 km long — with dipterocarp forests sheltering 130 mammal species. The park entrance is a five-minute walk from the village. Your guide teaches you to read the forest: orangutan nests in the trees, macaque tracks, gibbon calls announcing their territory in the early morning.

The return trail follows the Bohorok River to a more accessible part of the park where semi-wild orangutans congregate — former residents of the rehabilitation centre founded in 1973, they are used to human presence but not domesticated. You can watch them building their nests in the trees in the late afternoon. Do not feed them and keep your bag closed: they are perfectly capable of opening it. Long-tailed macaques and Thomas leaf monkeys (endemic to Sumatra) are even easier to spot than orangutans.

activities.text: River tubing on the Bohorok is the post-trek tradition in Bukit Lawang — you sit on inflatable inner tubes tied together and ride 45 minutes of rapids between jungle cliffs. It is how the guides bring their groups back to the village after the trek.

visits.text: Berastagi sits at 1,300 m altitude in the Karo Highlands — after the humid heat of Bukit Lawang, the change in air is immediate. The mountain road offers views of the cabbage, tomato and passion fruit plantations that have made the region's agricultural reputation. Settle in and head out to see Mount Sinabung from the viewpoints north of town: this 2,460 m volcano is one of Indonesia's most active and its plumes of smoke are visible in clear weather from Berastagi. It erupted in 2010 after 400 years of dormancy, then again in 2013–2014 with significant damage to nearby villages.

Berastagi's fruit market, along the main street, is the best in the region: marquisa (passion fruit), mountain strawberries, pepino, salak and above all the apples grown on Sibayak's slopes. Batak Karo vendors wear the sarong and traditional woven hat. It is also the place to buy the fresh flowers that have made the town famous — a cabbage-shaped monument was even erected in 1972 to celebrate the area's agricultural wealth.

activities.text: Plan your Sibayak ascent for tomorrow morning, and spend the afternoon soaking in the Lau Debuk-Debuk hot springs, 5 km north of Berastagi towards the volcano's geothermal sources. These small pools heated by Sibayak's underground activity are frequented by locals unwinding after work in the fields. The water smells faintly of sulphur but is perfect for recovering after the forest days.

visits.text: Mount Sibayak (2,212 m) is Indonesia's most accessible volcano — its name comes from the Batak Karo word for 'King's Mountain'. It went extinct over a century ago (last eruption 1881) but remains geothermally active: as you approach the crater, you can hear the fumaroles hissing and smell the sulphur. The 3.5 km trail from the starting point north of Berastagi takes about 3 hours return. Set off between 5 and 6 am to reach the summit at sunrise — on a clear day you can see Sinabung smoking to the east and the Karo Highlands stretching to the horizon. Bring a jacket: it is chilly.

Sipiso-Piso Waterfall, 24 km north of Berastagi near the village of Tongging, plunges 120 metres from the plateau down to Lake Toba stretching below. The lookout above the falls offers the first view of the lake — at this altitude, the immensity of the lake inside its ancient volcanic crater begins to reveal itself. Take the path down if you want to approach the base of the falls (allow 30 minutes for the descent).

activities.text: Detour to Lingga, a Batak Karo village a few kilometres northwest of Berastagi, to see traditional houses that are still inhabited. These large dwellings with curved saddle-back roofs, their facades decorated in red, black and white motifs representing sky, earth and the underworld, house several families. A local guide can explain how the space is organised and the social rules governing Karo communal life.

map_keywords: Ferry Parapat Samosir Island Lake Toba

visits.text: Lake Toba is the world's largest volcanic caldera — it formed during a cataclysmic eruption roughly 74,000 years ago that expelled 2,800 km³ of material and is thought to have caused a global cooling of several degrees. The lake measures 100 km long by 30 km wide, reaching a depth of 505 m. From Parapat, a ferry crosses in 30 minutes to Tuk-Tuk on the Samosir peninsula. Settle in on the lake side — guesthouses with direct lake access are plentiful and the view over the calm water ringed by forested hills is especially beautiful at sunset.

The village of Ambarita, 2 km north of Tuk-Tuk, is famous for its stone chairs — an eighteenth-century Batak Toba court where kings delivered their verdicts. The accused sat on the small chair, the king on the larger one. According to tradition, those found guilty of serious crimes could be executed on the flat stones nearby. The staging is spectacular and local guides recount these stories with disarming matter-of-factness.

activities.text: Rent a scooter for the evening and explore Samosir's east coast. The Batak Toba are predominantly Protestant Christians — a legacy of nineteenth-century German Lutheran missions. You will see churches in every village, amid Batak tombs painted white, red and yellow. Batak culture is one of Indonesia's most complex: patrilineal clans, marriage alliance systems, gondang music, hand-woven ulos cloth — an introduction tonight, and a deeper exploration tomorrow.

map_keywords: Lake Toba swim kayak Tuk Tuk

visits.text: Pusuk Buhit (1,978 m), on the isthmus connecting Samosir to the mainland, is the sacred mountain of the Batak Toba — according to their mythology, this is where the first ancestor of the Batak people lived. The climb on foot or by scooter to the first viewpoints is one of the most beautiful on Samosir: terraced rice paddies step down to the lake's edge, and on a clear day you can see the entire caldera. The sulphurous hot springs of Pangururan, at the foot of Pusuk Buhit, are heated by the caldera's volcanic activity.

Bukit Holbung, a hill reachable by scooter from Tuk-Tuk, offers a 360° panorama of the lake, Samosir's hills and the mainland mountains. It is one of the island's least visited viewpoints — you will often find only a few grazing cows for company. Arrive in the early morning before the mist rises.

activities.text: Swim in Lake Toba from your guesthouse or kayak to the small coves of the Tuk-Tuk peninsula. The water is fresh, calm and remarkably clean for such a large lake. Surface temperature is around 25–26°C. If you prefer to stay on land, most lakeside restaurants serve grilled ikan mas (golden carp) caught that morning — the local speciality to try with andaliman, a Sichuan pepper grown around the lake.

visits.text: The day starts before dawn: catch the ferry from Tuk-Tuk back to Parapat (30 min) then allow 7 to 8 hours of driving to Bukittinggi via the Sumatran highlands. The road crosses landscapes of rice paddies, rubber plantations and roadside markets. Arrival in mid-afternoon. If you opt for an overnight bus from Parapat (departing around 8 pm), you arrive in Bukittinggi at about 6 am and gain a full day of sightseeing.

The Jam Gadang has dominated Bukittinggi's central square since 1926 — it was a gift from the Dutch Queen Wilhelmina to her city secretary. This 26 m tower was built without any metal framework or cement: a mixture of limestone, egg whites and sand. Its four dials display the number 4 as 'IIII' rather than 'IV' — a quirk it shares with Big Ben in London, because the mechanisms were made by the same German manufacturer, Bernard Vortmann, in Recklinghausen. The roof has changed three times: European style during the Dutch era, Japanese style under the 1942–1945 occupation, Minangkabau style (the buffalo horns) after independence.

Sianok Canyon (Ngarai Sianok), at the southwestern edge of town, plunges 100 to 120 m over 15 km. It is not a river-carved canyon but a tectonic fault — the same fault that runs the entire length of Sumatra. From the Panorama Park lookout, the view is vertiginous: sheer cliffs fall onto rice paddies crossed by a winding river, framed by forests and hills. Early morning, the mist filling the canyon is spectacular.

The Japanese tunnels (Lobang Jepang), dug 40 m beneath Sianok Canyon by forced Indonesian labourers during the 1942–1945 occupation, extend for 1,470 m. This military network served as a headquarters, weapons depot and shelter for Japanese soldiers of the 25th Army based in Bukittinggi. The interior has explanatory panels on the occupation. It is dark and cool — bring a jacket.

activities.text: Dine at the evening markets around the Jam Gadang for your first immersion in Minangkabau cuisine. Beef rendang (meat braised for hours in coconut milk and spices until caramelised), sate Padang (skewers with thick spicy sauce), gulai ayam (chicken curry with turmeric) — this cooking is the foundation of the nasi Padang you will find everywhere in the region.

map_keywords: Museum Rumah Kelahiran Bung Hatta Bukittinggi

visits.text: Pagaruyung Palace, 40 km southeast of Bukittinggi in the Batusangkar plain, is the reconstruction of the Minangkabau royal palace that burned down in 2007 after being struck by lightning — the seventeenth-century original had itself already burned and been rebuilt. This monument is an architectural statement: three tiers of upswept roofs evoking buffalo horns, each level decorated with floral patterns painted in red, black and gold. The Minangkabau are matrilineal — property and family names pass through the women, a social organisation that is extremely rare at this scale.

Koto Gadang, Bukittinggi's silversmith village, is reachable on foot from Sianok Canyon via a trail or by minibus. This village has produced filigree silver jewellery for generations — you can watch artisans at work in their family workshops. The technique of twisted and soldered silver wire is unique to this region. It is also the birthplace of several figures of Indonesian independence.

activities.text: Explore Pasar Atas, Bukittinggi's large daily market, to buy batik textiles, embroidery, dried chillies and spices. It is one of the busiest markets in West Sumatra — vendors come from across the regency with their agricultural produce. Then head down to Pasar Bawah for artisanal souvenirs and Chinatown for traditional Chinese medicines.

visits.text: Harau Valley (Lembah Harau) reveals itself gradually as you leave Payakumbuh: granite and red sandstone cliffs surge up 80 to 300 m, encircling chessboard rice paddies of a luminous green. The rocks are 40 million years old according to geologists, and the valley is part of an official geopark. Dubbed 'Indonesia's Yosemite' by the global climbing community, Harau has 300 routes on its vertical walls. Even without climbing, the sight of the cliffs at the end of the day when the light turns them red is reason enough for the detour.

Sarasah Bunta waterfall, the most spectacular on the site, tumbles directly from the edge of a cliff into a pool surrounded by moss-covered boulders. It was officially inaugurated in 1926 by a Dutch colonial administrator — a plaque still testifies to this in the rock. Four rivers cross the valley, feeding seven catalogued waterfalls. Allow 5 minutes' walk between each.

activities.text: Walk or ride a scooter through the valley in the late afternoon to watch farmers at work in the rice paddies. Water buffaloes still plough the fields with their drivers — a scene of Minangkabau agricultural life barely touched by time. Rumah gadang houses with buffalo-horn roofs dot the valley floor. Macaques and gibbons still live in the forests along the cliff edges.

map_keywords: Pantai Air Manis Padang Malin Kundang stone

visits.text: Pantai Air Manis (Freshwater Beach), 20 minutes from central Padang, is the setting for West Sumatra's most famous legend: the tale of Malin Kundang, the ungrateful son who disowned his poor mother after becoming rich. Legend has it that his mother cursed him and his ship ran aground — the rock formation on the beach resembling a prostrate man is said to be his petrified remains. The beach itself is pleasant in the morning before the crowds arrive.

The Museum Adityawarman, in a building modelled on a large-scale gadang house, brings together the ethnographic collections of the entire West Sumatra province: Minangkabau textiles, gold ornaments, musical instruments, traditional weapons. The museum is named after Adityawarman, a fourteenth-century ruler who reigned over West Sumatra and whose Sanskrit inscriptions are among the oldest on the island. Allow 1h30.

The old port of Muaro and its colonial quarter are the most authentic spot in Padang to feel the city's trading history. Eighteenth-century Dutch warehouses line the Batang Arau — some are still used to store cinnamon and coffee awaiting shipment. The Siti Nurbaya Bridge, lit up at night, links the old port to the hill where the tomb of the heroine from the classic Indonesian novel of the same name is said to lie.

activities.text: Lunch at a genuine rumah makan Padang to experience the hidang service in its place of origin: the moment you sit down, the waiter arrives with arms stacked with 12 to 14 plates which he spreads across the table. You only pay for what you touch. Rendang, gulai ikan, sate Padang, daun singkong — each dish is cooked from family recipes handed down for generations. This is the original version of what you find in Padang restaurants worldwide.

visits.text: The Baiturrahman Grand Mosque is the first place to see in Banda Aceh — and its condensed history tells that of the entire city. Burned by the Dutch during their 1873 attack (in which they lost their commander, General Köhler, killed by an Acehnese sniper), it was rebuilt from 1879 by the same occupiers as a gesture of reconciliation. Completed in 1881, with Indian-style black domes and 35 m white minarets, it remained standing during the 26 December 2004 tsunami while the buildings around it collapsed — hundreds of survivors took shelter by climbing onto the domes to escape the waves.

The Aceh Tsunami Museum, designed by architect Ridwan Kamil (later governor of West Java), is a 2,500 m² structure over four levels whose long curved facades are covered in geometric low-reliefs. The entrance takes you through the Lorong Tsunami — a dark corridor where water streams down both sides producing a low rumble. Then comes the hall of names: 40,000 victims from Aceh province inscribed on the walls. The 26 December 2004 earthquake reached 9.3 on the Richter scale — one of the most powerful ever recorded. The total death toll across the Indian Ocean exceeded 230,000 in twelve countries.

activities.text: In the evening, visit the Gunongan, a set of pleasure baths built in the seventeenth century for the queen of Sultan Iskandar Muda — terraces stepping down in a flower shape on the banks of the Aceh River. It is one of the few remaining architectural traces of the Sultanate of Aceh, one of Southeast Asia's most powerful thalassocracies in the seventeenth century, which controlled the pepper trade and the entrance to the Strait of Malacca.

map_keywords: PLTD Apung ship museum Banda Aceh

visits.text: The PLTD Apung 1 is a 2,600-tonne power-generating vessel that the 26 December 2004 tsunami tore from its dock and carried 3 km inland before depositing it on top of two houses in a residential neighbourhood — whose occupants are probably still underneath. This is not a conventional museum but an involuntary monument to chaos. You climb on deck to grasp the scale: this metal colossus sitting among ordinary houses is the best measure of the tsunami's hydraulic force. A semi-circular wall with bas-reliefs illustrating the scene surrounds the site.

The Aceh State Museum (Museum Negeri Aceh) preserves the sultanate's historical collections: sets of weapons, royal costumes, musical instruments, gold jewellery. A section is devoted to inscriptions in Old Malay and Arabic that bear witness to Aceh's role as the first region of Indonesia to convert to Islam, as early as the thirteenth century.

Lampuuk Beach, 15 km southwest of Banda Aceh, is the most beautiful on the Acehnese coast: 3 km of white sand facing the Indian Ocean. Before the tsunami, it was a popular beach. The 2004 wave destroyed everything — the site has been entirely rebuilt with international aid. Several mosques in the area survived, including the Rahmatullah Mosque in Lhoknga, often photographed standing amid the ruins — an iconic image of the disaster. On this final day, Banda Aceh is 20 km away and Sultan Iskandar Muda Airport is within easy reach.

activities.text: Final free morning in Banda Aceh to buy local specialities: kopi Aceh, Indonesia's finest coffee roasted the old-fashioned way in wajan (iron pans) with butter and sugar, kasab embroidery (needlework on red and gold velvet) and spices. Cafés around the Baiturrahman Mosque open early — ordering a kopi sanger (coffee with condensed milk) is an Acehnese ritual.

seo.h1_title: Northern Sumatra: Orangutans, Lake Toba and Tsunami Memorials in 14 Days
seo.intro_paragraph: Start from Medan and plunge into the primary forests of Gunung Leuser where 130 mammal species share 7,927 km² of jungle — including the Sumatran orangutan, one of only two surviving populations on Earth. Climb the sulphur-steaming Sibayak (2,212 m), cross Lake Toba — the world's largest volcanic caldera, formed 74,000 years ago — before ending in Banda Aceh, a city that survived the 2004 tsunami which killed 160,000 Acehnese.
seo.seo_hook: Orangutans in primary forest, a giant volcanic caldera, Minangkabau rice paddies and memorials of the 2004 tsunami.
featured_list: Bukit Lawang: trek through Gunung Leuser National Park, a UNESCO site, to observe Sumatran orangutans in the wild
Berastagi & Sibayak: climb an active 2,212 m volcano with sulphurous fumaroles and views of Sinabung
Lake Toba & Samosir: sail across the world's largest volcanic caldera and experience Batak Toba culture
Banda Aceh: Tsunami Museum (Ridwan Kamil's architecture), PLTD Apung and the Grand Mosque that survived 2004

places_taglines.ID::medan: Indonesia's third-largest city, a colonial crossroads of Malay, Chinese and Indian cultures since the 19th century
places_taglines.ID::bukit-lawang: Trekking village on the Bohorok River, gateway to the primary forests of Gunung Leuser
places_taglines.ID::berastagi: Highland retreat at 1,300 m, at the foot of the active Sibayak, heart of Batak Karo culture
places_taglines.ID::tuk-tuk: Peninsula on Samosir Island in Lake Toba, centre of Christian Batak Toba culture
places_taglines.ID::bukittinggi: Cultural capital of West Sumatra at 930 m, cradle of the Minangkabau people
places_taglines.ID::harau-valley: 300 m granite canyon dubbed Indonesia's Yosemite, with seven waterfalls and 300 climbing routes
places_taglines.ID::padang: Capital of West Sumatra, birthplace of Nasi Padang and spicy Minangkabau cuisine exported worldwide
places_taglines.ID::banda-aceh: City that survived the 9.3 earthquake and 2004 tsunami that killed 160,000 Acehnese

highlights: Gunung Leuser National Park: 7,927 km² of UNESCO primary forest, one of only two habitats of the Sumatran orangutan
Lake Toba: the world's largest volcanic caldera, 100 km long, formed by an eruption 74,000 years ago
Jam Gadang: 26 m tower built in 1926 without cement, with the same clockwork mechanisms as Big Ben in London
PLTD Apung Banda Aceh: 2,600-tonne vessel carried 3 km inland by the 26 December 2004 tsunami

itineraries.title: Sulawesi: Bunaken Reefs, Toraja Highlands and Wakatobi Lagoons in 20 Days
itineraries.subtitle: From Bunaken's marine park to the funeral cliffs of Tana Toraja, through highland volcanoes and Wakatobi's record-breaking reefs — the grand tour of Indonesia's K-shaped island
itineraries.notes: This itinerary requires several domestic flights: Tomohon→Gorontalo (flight or 5h drive), Palu→Makassar (flight), Makassar→Selayar (flight), Selayar→Wakatobi (flight). The Togean→Palu leg is by ferry. Ferries from Gorontalo to the Togean Islands depart on Tuesdays and Fridays — check schedules in advance. Tana Toraja: the grand Rambu Solo funeral ceremonies take place from June to August — the best time to visit but also the busiest.

map_keywords: Ban Hin Kiong Temple Manado Chinatown
Museum Negeri Provinsi Sulawesi Utara Manado

visits.text: Welcome to Manado, capital of North Sulawesi and gateway to some of Indonesia's richest underwater treasures. The city's name likely derives from the Portuguese manadas, meaning "cluster of offshore islands" — a hint at its seafaring calling since the 15th century. The Portuguese landed here in 1511, followed by the Spanish, the Dutch and the British, all vying for control of this trading post over two centuries. Present-day Manado is home to around 460,000 people and proudly calls itself the "City of a Thousand Churches" — no exaggeration: Christian places of worship line every other intersection, a legacy of the Dutch Protestant mission in the 17th century. Stroll along the Boulevard, the city's waterfront esplanade, to soak in the local atmosphere before diving into its streets.

The Ban Hin Kiong Temple, in Manado's Chinatown district, is recognised as the oldest Taoist temple in eastern Indonesia. Founded in 1819 during the Qing dynasty, it was originally built from bamboo before being rebuilt in stone. Its dragon statues, pagodas and monks in orange robes make it a peaceful cultural stop amid the city's bustle. Come early in the morning to catch the surrounding market in full swing and a more contemplative atmosphere.

The North Sulawesi State Museum (Museum Negeri Provinsi Sulawesi Utara) showcases prehistoric artefacts, ceremonial costumes of the Minahasa people, traditional musical instruments, and objects from the Dutch colonial era and the Japanese occupation during World War II. If you only visit one thing in Manado before heading out to the islands, this is the place — it puts everything you're about to discover into context.

activities.text: In the evening, head to Pasar Jengki, the night market known as "Manado's kitchen". This market, rooted in local life for generations, brims with fresh seafood, spices and classic Minahasa dishes: tinutuan (rice porridge with mixed vegetables), cakalang fufu (wood-smoked tuna) and fiery preparations made with rica-rica (red chilli paste). Minahasa cuisine is reputed as one of the spiciest in all of Indonesia — fair warning if your chilli tolerance is low.

map_keywords: Bahowo pier Manado boat to Bunaken

visits.text: Depart by 8 AM from Bahowo or Molas pier towards Bunaken Island, a 30-minute boat ride away. Bunaken National Marine Park was officially established in 1991 — one of Indonesia's earliest marine parks. It covers 890 km², of which 97% is marine environment, and encompasses five islands: Bunaken, Manado Tua (an extinct volcano rising in a perfect cone over 600 m), Siladen, Mantehage and Nain. The park harbours 390 coral species and around 2,000 fish species — more than 70% of all known fish species in Indonesian waters. The geological peculiarity: the absence of a continental shelf means the seabed plunges directly from a few metres to over 1,800 m. These sheer vertical walls of 25 to 50 metres are what made Bunaken famous.

Manado Tua, the volcanic silhouette standing out on the horizon from the boat, is worth the trip on its own. The island has been inhabited for generations by fishing families whose ancestors fled the volcano during an ancient eruption — hence its name "Old Manado". The Muka Gereja dive site (literally "facing the church") at the foot of the volcano is one of the most spectacular in the park: massive coral heads, schools of parrotfish and a strong current suited to experienced divers.

activities.text: Diving or snorkelling on the Lekuan Walls (Lekuan I, II and III) — Bunaken's most iconic sites. These vertical walls drop over 100 metres and are covered in giant sponges, sea fans and colourful hard corals. Lekuan I alone hosts about a dozen green turtles that divers regularly encounter between 5 and 30 metres. Napoleon wrasse (up to 2 m), eagle rays, whitetip reef sharks and schools of barracuda patrol these walls. For beginners or snorkellers, Lekuan III offers the same species in shallow areas. Operators based on the island offer packages of 2 to 3 dives per day with full equipment.

practical_info.booking: Advance booking from Manado recommended
practical_info.note: Best visibility from April to October

visits.text: Spend your second day at Bunaken exploring the less crowded sites. Mandolin Point, named after the "harmony" divers feel here, is an outstanding wall that reveals something different at every depth — spectacular at 5 m just as much as at 35 m. Fukui Point is the only site in Bunaken that offers a gently sloping reef instead of a vertical wall: ideal for snorkelling and for watching schools of bumphead parrotfish grazing on hard corals near the surface. In 1997, it was in the lava tubes of Manado Tua that the coelacanth — a "living fossil" that first appeared 400 million years ago — was filmed in situ for the first time anywhere in the world. If you're lucky during deep dives around Manado Tua, you're in its habitat.

Siladen Island, the smallest and least known of the park, lies 8 km east of Bunaken. Its south-western wall is one of the most colourful in the park, home to swarms of damselfish, rare nudibranchs and schools of snappers. The shallow reef surface is perfect for calm snorkelling with crystal-clear visibility. If you're staying overnight on Bunaken, Siladen is typically the afternoon destination.

activities.text: Night dive on Bunaken's fringing reef. The nocturnal scene reveals an entirely different cast of creatures: octopuses hunting in the open, coral crabs, moray eels on patrol and pygmy seahorses hiding in whip corals. The bioluminescence of certain corals makes Bunaken's night dives especially photogenic. Bring your own torch or check that your operator provides one.

Take a walk through Bunaken village to observe the daily life of fishermen and families living within the park. Much of the population still depends on traditional fishing and seaweed farming — some have since shifted to dive tourism. The sunrise from the island's beach, facing the cone of Manado Tua, is one of the most striking views of the entire trip.

map_keywords: Tangkoko Batuangus Nature Reserve North Sulawesi

visits.text: The Tangkoko-Batuangus Nature Reserve, at the foot of Mount Dua Saudara (1,361 m), covers 8,718 hectares of lowland rainforest — one of the largest intact forests in the Minahasa region. The reserve was established in 1919 to protect its endemic species. Today it shelters at least 127 mammal species, 233 bird species and 104 reptile and amphibian species, the vast majority of which are endemic to Sulawesi. The most endangered include the crested black macaque (Macaca nigra), with only around 5,500 individuals remaining across the entire island, and the spectral tarsier, the world's smallest primate. Between 1978 and 1993, hunting had driven the macaque population down by 75% — this reserve is their last viable refuge.

The black volcanic beach of Batuputih, at the reserve entrance, is one of the region's most unusual landscapes. The dark sand contrasts sharply with the dense forest and the turquoise waters of the gulf. Flights of knobbed hornbills (Rhyticeros cassidix) — the emblem of South Sulawesi province — regularly sweep across the canopy above the beach. If you're here at sunrise, macaque troops often descend to the shoreline to forage in the shallows.

activities.text: Trek through the reserve with a local guide — mandatory for park access. The late-afternoon outing (departing around 3:30 PM) is the best option: you first observe black macaques in full social mode — troops can number up to 100 individuals, and some are so accustomed to researchers that they'll let you approach within a metre — then search for tarsiers as dusk falls. The spectral tarsier measures 10 to 15 cm and weighs 80 to 100 grams: its eyes, larger than its brain, allow it to see in total darkness. Never shine a torch directly into their eyes. Local guides from Batuputih Bawah village will accompany you for a 2- to 3-hour walk.

practical_info.booking: Mandatory guide available at the village entrance
practical_info.note: Arrive before dusk for the tarsiers

activities.text: If you stay overnight at Tangkoko, the early-morning trek the next day offers the best chances of spotting knobbed hornbills in the canopy and macaques returning from the coast. Some operators also run traditional canoe trips through the mangroves to observe herons, kingfishers and monitor lizards. Ask at your accommodation the evening before.

map_keywords: Tomohon Extreme Market Pasar Extrem Sulawesi

visits.text: Tomohon, nicknamed the "City of Flowers", is a highland town nestled between two active volcanoes — Lokon (1,580 m) and Mahawu (1,324 m) — at roughly 800 m elevation. The air is cool, the gardens are lush and the views across the Minahasa highlands are stunning. Start with the Pasar Extrem, the most unusual — and most controversial — market in Sulawesi. This traditional Minahasa market sells fruit, vegetables, spices and local treats like cucur (sweet fritters made with palm sugar). It earned its nickname from the exotic meat section — wild boar, bats, snakes and other local animals — reflecting the ancestral food traditions of a mountain-dwelling people. Come with an open mind and a ready stomach.

Lake Linow, a few kilometres from Tomohon, is a volcanic lake whose colour shifts throughout the day: emerald green, Prussian blue, milky white and sometimes deep red. This phenomenon is caused by changing sulphur levels and lighting conditions. Several lakeside cafés let you sit back and watch these colour changes unfold over a cup of highland coffee. The steam rising intermittently from the surface is a reminder that the geology here is very much alive.

activities.text: Hike to the crater of Mount Mahawu. The ascent from the car park takes about an hour on a concrete-step trail — suitable for beginners. At the summit, a green crater lake sits amid dense tropical vegetation. On a clear day, the view stretches to Manado, Bunaken, Lembeh Island and the Minahasa volcanic chain. Leave early in the morning for the best visibility before clouds build up.

In the late afternoon, drive to Lake Tondano, the largest lake in North Sulawesi, about 30 minutes from Tomohon along a mountain road. Sitting at 600 m elevation inside the caldera of the ancient Burpa volcano, its shores are lined with fish restaurants serving nike (a species endemic to the lake). Order grilled nike fish with rice and chilli sauce while watching the sunset over the mountains — the most local table you'll find anywhere in the region.

visits.text: Gorontalo, capital of the province bearing the same name, is a city often overlooked by travellers rushing to reach the Togean Islands. That's a mistake. The town grew around a federation of five pre-colonial kingdoms before attracting Portuguese interest in the 16th century. Otanaha Fortress, perched on a hill overlooking the bay, is one of the few surviving traces of that presence: three round stone towers built in the 15th century by the Portuguese, with panoramic views over the city and Lake Limboto below. Climb the steps in the late afternoon for the golden light.

Lake Limboto, visible from the fortress tower, stretches across the heart of Gorontalo province. From the Tower of Majesty viewpoint (Menara Keagungan), the vista over the lake and surrounding fishing villages is especially beautiful at sunset. The lake is shallow (2 to 4 m) but rich in local aquatic biodiversity, and its banks are home to communities of traditional fishermen.

activities.text: Snorkelling or diving at Olele Marine Park, a 30-minute drive from town. This site, still largely unknown to international tourists, is often compared to a surrealist Salvador Dalí canvas underwater: its bizarrely shaped coral formations, giant sponges and canyon-like reefs are unique in the entire region. Visibility is excellent and currents are generally mild. Local operators run trips from Olele beach.

practical_info.booking: Operators available on site

activities.text: In season (mainly November to April), the whale sharks of Botubarani are the region's most extraordinary attraction. At Botubarani, 20 minutes east of town, whale sharks — the largest fish in the world, reaching up to 12 metres — come to feed at the surface just a few dozen metres from shore, drawn in by shrimp brought by local boats. The experience involves swimming alongside them from a boat or from the beach. Their presence is not guaranteed — check with local operators the day before.

practical_info.note: Peak season November–April. Not guaranteed outside season.

visits.text: Spend the morning exploring Gorontalo city before the evening departure. The floating Baiturrahim Mosque, built on a platform at the water's edge, is one of the city's most photographed landmarks. Its neo-classical white-and-green architecture reflects beautifully in the waters of Tomini Bay on a clear day. Dress respectfully if you wish to enter.

If time allows, a boat trip to Saronde Island, about an hour north of Gorontalo, offers a day on a white-sand beach surrounded by turquoise water with very few visitors. Alternatively, head to the famous Pulo Cinta (Love Island), a heart-shaped island perched on a sandbar in Tomini Bay — one of Sulawesi's best-known romantic attractions. Local operators run morning transfers from Gorontalo harbour.

activities.text: In the evening, board the KM Tuna Tomini ferry from Gorontalo harbour. The ferry departs on Tuesdays and Fridays around 6 PM bound for Wakai in the Togean Islands. The crossing takes approximately 12 hours — bring a hammock, a light sleeping bag and some food. The overnight passage across Tomini Bay is an experience in itself: the Sulawesi sky, far from any light pollution, is breathtaking. Arrival at Wakai in the early morning hours.

practical_info.note: Departures Tue. & Fri. — confirm local schedules before booking

visits.text: Arrival at Wakai, on Batudaka Island, in the Togean Islands — an archipelago of 56 islands and islets in the middle of the Gulf of Tomini, designated a National Park in 2004. Sailors describe the Gulf of Tomini as one of the calmest deep-water bays in the world, sheltered by the two arms of Sulawesi that flank it. The Togean Islands feature all three types of coral reef within a single area — fringing reefs, barrier reefs and atolls — a combination found nowhere else on Earth according to marine biologists. The archipelago supports roughly 262 coral species and 596 fish species. Settle into one of the small coastal bungalows on Kadidiri or Togean Island — accommodation is basic but the reefs are accessible right from the beach.

Lake Mariona (also known as Jellyfish Lake) on Togean Island is one of the most improbable experiences you can have in Indonesia. This ancient marine lake, formed in a limestone sinkhole flooded by seawater, is home to two species of jellyfish that have lost their stinging cells through evolution — having no predators left in this enclosed lake. You can swim (without fins, to avoid harming them) among millions of translucent jellyfish drifting slowly around you. It is one of the very few places in the world where this is possible.

activities.text: Snorkelling or diving on the Togean reefs. Unlike Bunaken, diving here is still largely wild and uncrowded. The best-known site is the wreck of an American B-24 bomber from World War II, which went down on 3 May 1945 during a mission over Sulawesi. The 17-metre aircraft rests intact at 18 m depth on a sandy bed, its machine guns still visible. Hundreds of bigeye trevally swirl around it constantly. Operators on Kadidiri Island arrange dive trips to this site and other reefs.

practical_info.booking: Book with operators on the island

map_keywords: Bajo Sea Gypsies village Pulau Papan Togean
Una Una volcano dive site Togean

visits.text: Don't leave the Togean Islands without visiting a Bajo village. The Bajo (or Badjao) are known as the "sea gypsies" — a semi-nomadic people, 25,000 of whom live in the Togean Islands in stilt houses connected by wooden walkways above the water. They are renowned for their extraordinary breath-holding abilities, inherited from generations of open-water fishing. Pulau Papan, connected by a 1.3-km bridge, is the most accessible Bajo settlement in the archipelago. Approach with respect: avoid photographing without permission.

Una Una Island, off the beaten tourist track, is the wildest in the archipelago. Its active volcano forms a perfect cone visible from afar. The dive sites around Una Una are among the most diverse in the Togean Islands: giant sponges, massive sea fans, dense schools of trevally and barracuda, and reefs less affected by coral bleaching than sites in the south of the archipelago. Few operators service Una Una — ask around as soon as you arrive at Wakai.

activities.text: Free day on the Togean beaches. The white-sand beaches of the surrounding small islets — reachable in 15 to 30 minutes by boat from Kadidiri — are among the most pristine in Indonesia. Bring your own provisions as there's nothing on the islets. Ask your accommodation to arrange a custom boat tour to the most remote reefs and beaches. This is the place to switch off completely — some lodges have no Wi-Fi and few have electricity around the clock.

map_keywords: Lore Lindu National Park entrance Palu

visits.text: Palu, capital of Central Sulawesi, is a transit city between the Togean Islands and the rest of the island. Take the return ferry to Ampana or Gorontalo depending on available schedules, then reach Palu by road or by flight from Gorontalo. The city sits at the head of a long, narrow bay — one of Indonesia's most distinctive micro-bays, ringed by mountains that block rainfall and give it one of the lowest precipitation rates in the country. Palu was severely hit by the 2018 earthquake and tsunami — the scars are still visible in some neighbourhoods. A sober walk along the bay shore helps convey the brutal volcanic geology of Sulawesi.

Lore Lindu National Park, about an hour southeast of Palu, is a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve spanning 217,000 hectares of montane tropical forest. It shelters enigmatic megaliths — anthropomorphic stone statues dating from the Bronze Age whose origin and purpose remain a mystery — along with 77 endemic bird species, Tonkean macaques and Dian's tarsiers. If you have time in Palu, a half-day excursion to the megaliths of the Bada Valley is one of the most otherworldly experiences in Central Sulawesi.

activities.text: Evening stroll on Taman Ria beach. This Palu waterfront is the locals' promenade, lined with street-food vendors, fish barbecues and a laid-back atmosphere. Try the local ikan bakar — whole fish grilled over coals, served with rice and chilli sauce — before getting ready for tomorrow's flight to Makassar.

map_keywords: Fort Rotterdam Makassar Dutch colonial fort
Masjid Amirul Mukminin floating mosque Makassar

visits.text: Makassar (formerly Ujung Pandang) is Indonesia's fifth-largest city and the capital of South Sulawesi — a maritime port city that wrote much of the archipelago's seafaring history. Fort Rotterdam is the city's most iconic landmark: this sea-turtle-shaped fort (Benteng Penyu) was originally built in 1545 by King Tunipalangga of the Gowa dynasty — the tenth ruler of the kingdom. The current outer walls of 6-metre-high volcanic stone were rebuilt by Dutch Admiral Cornelis Speelman after his conquest in 1667, and named after his hometown in the Netherlands. The fort later served as a colonial prison: this is where Javanese national hero Prince Diponegoro was imprisoned for 25 years until his death in 1855. Inside, the La Galigo Museum displays prehistoric artefacts, Bugis costumes and objects from the great Bugis-Makassar oral epic tradition.

Pantai Losari is Makassar's waterfront promenade: 1 km of seafront with no actual sandy beach, but plenty of street-food vendors, the floating Masjid Amirul Mukminin mosque, and crowds of locals gathering every evening to watch the sunset over the Makassar Strait. It's the social heartbeat of the city. Plan to be there around 5:30 PM for the golden hour.

Paotere Harbour, 3 km north of Fort Rotterdam, is the traditional port of the pinisi — the tall twin-masted sailing ships built for centuries by the Bugis shipwrights of Sulawesi. These wooden schooners still carry goods across the Indonesian archipelago today. Come early in the morning to see crews loading and unloading, brightly painted hulls lined up along the quay.

activities.text: Dinner at one of the Losari waterfront restaurants or in the alleys behind the fort. Makassar's specialities are not to be missed: coto makassar (thick, spicy beef and offal soup served with ketupat — pressed rice cakes), konro bakar (grilled beef ribs in a dark spicy sauce), and es pisang ijo (green banana ice cream, the city's signature dessert). These are dishes you won't find at the same level outside Makassar.

visits.text: Day trip to Rammang-Rammang, 40 km north of Makassar in the Maros district. This is the second-largest karst landscape in the world — after Guangxi in China — with black limestone towers of 50 to 300 metres surging from rice paddies and mangroves. Traditional boats take you along the Pute River as it winds at the foot of sheer, vegetation-draped cliffs. The atmosphere feels like a fantasy film set. A network of paths runs through Rammang-Rammang village, home to families of fishermen and farmers, with no other tourism in sight.

The Leang-Leang caves, within the same Maros karst massif, hold some of the oldest cave paintings in the world. In 2021, researchers dated one of the warty pig depictions in Leang Tedongnge cave to at least 45,500 years ago — the oldest known figurative painting to date. Hand stencils and animals in red ochre are still visible on the walls. A guide is essential and some caves require special access permission.

activities.text: Return to Makassar in the evening with a stop at Bantimurung — dubbed the "Kingdom of Butterflies" by naturalist Alfred Russel Wallace, who worked there between 1856 and 1857. This national park is home to at least 250 butterfly species, including 20 that are protected. Wallace, who developed his theory of natural selection independently of Darwin, described Bantimurung as one of his favourite study sites in Asia. An on-site museum traces his time there.

map_keywords: Kete Kesu traditional village Tana Toraja
Bori Parinding megalith site Tana Toraja

visits.text: Arrival in Rantepao, the main town of Tana Toraja, after a roughly 6-hour mountain drive from Makassar — a magnificent route through terraced rice fields, bamboo forests and the highlands of South Sulawesi. The Toraja are an Austronesian highland people numbering over one million. The word toraja comes from the Bugis to riaja: "people of the uplands". The Dutch colonial government officially gave them this name in 1909. Their ancestral belief system, Aluk To Dolo (the Way of the Ancestors), structures all social life around the cult of the dead. Most Toraja are Christian today, but the ancestral rites continue with undiminished intensity.

Visit Kete Kesu village, 4 km from Rantepao — one of the best-preserved and most accessible tongkonan villages in the region. Tongkonan are the ancestral houses of the Toraja: built on stilts, their upward-curving roofs — shaped like an upside-down boat — are instantly recognisable. Tradition holds that this design recalls the vessels on which the Toraja ancestors first arrived in Sulawesi. The walls are covered with pa'ssura — geometric motifs carved and painted in black, red, white and yellow, the four sacred Toraja colours, which "read" the family's history and cosmology. Facing the tongkonan stand the rice granaries (alang) of the same shape, forming an architectural ensemble found nowhere else in the world.

activities.text: Walk through the terraced rice fields around Rantepao at sunset. The Toraja rice paddies, carved into steep hillsides, are farmed using ancestral methods. This is also when the villages come alive — families returning from the fields, children playing in the lanes between the granaries. If you're invited to share a meal in someone's home, don't turn it down: Toraja cooking (pa'piong — meat cooked in bamboo — and highland Toraja arabica coffee) is outstanding.

map_keywords: Lemo cliff burial site tau tau Tana Toraja
Londa cave burial site Tana Toraja
Buntu Pune mountain viewpoint Tana Toraja

visits.text: Lemo is the most spectacular and most visited burial site in Tana Toraja. On a vertical limestone cliff face several dozen metres high, scores of burial niches have been carved by hand by to pande batu — specialist stone carvers hired by families to suspend their dead in the rock. The work could take months. In front of each tomb, a balcony holds the tau-tau: a wooden statue sculpted and painted in the likeness of the deceased, dressed in fresh clothes and gazing downward — toward the living. Toraja tradition holds that the dead are not truly gone until the funeral ceremony has been completed — until then, the body is kept in the ancestral house, dressed and fed as if merely ill. These customs can delay the ceremony by months or even years after biological death.

The Londa burial cave, a few kilometres from Lemo, is a complex of natural caverns where generations of Toraja families have been laid to rest. You enter with a kerosene lamp held by a local guide. Inside: ancestral coffins stacked one on top of another, skulls lined up on stone ledges, scattered bones. From the base of the cliff, a group of life-size tau-tau watches over the entrance from a carved wooden balcony. It is at once moving and profoundly unsettling to Western eyes.

Climb to the Buntu Pune or Batutumonga viewpoint for a single sweeping panorama of the entire Tana Toraja landscape: endless terraced rice fields, orange-and-red tongkonan scattered across the valleys, and clouds clinging to the forested peaks. This is the image you'll carry home from this trip.

activities.text: If you're here between June and August, you may be able to attend a Rambu Solo (or Rambu Tuka) funeral ceremony. This is the funeral season — after the rice harvest and before the new planting. These ceremonies can last from 3 days for a modest family to over two weeks for a noble one. Dozens to several hundred water buffaloes (karabao) are sacrificed — the horns, mounted on the front of the family tongkonan, testify to status and wealth. Thousands of people may attend. Foreign visitors are generally welcome provided they bring a symbolic gift (cigarettes, sugar, coffee) and dress modestly. Don't photograph without asking.

practical_info.note: June–August: peak ceremony season. Ask at local guesthouses for dates.

map_keywords: Kambira baby tree burial Tana Toraja
Palawa village tongkonan buffalo horns Tana Toraja
Tilanga natural pool swimming Tana Toraja

visits.text: The tree burial site of Kambira follows a unique custom: babies who die before cutting their first tooth (considered too "pure" to be buried in the ground) are placed in cavities hollowed into the trunks of living ficus trees. The tree gradually closes the wound around the small coffin, "absorbing" the child into its sap. Several dozen trees here have been used this way over multiple generations. The practice still continues today in some families.

Palawa village, 12 km north of Rantepao, is considered one of the best-preserved tongkonan villages in all of Toraja. The front of the main ancestral house is covered in rows of buffalo horns from sacrifices spanning several generations — the backbone of Toraja social status. Some façades display hundreds. The village has been listed among official tourist sites since 1987.

activities.text: Toraja arabica coffee tasting at one of the highland plantations around Rantepao. Tana Toraja coffee is grown between 1,000 and 1,500 m elevation on volcanic slopes — its earthy, spicy notes are unmistakable. It's exported worldwide but tastes best right here, freshly roasted over wood, at a warung overlooking the rice fields.

The natural pool of Tilanga, tucked into a wooded ravine 9 km from Rantepao, offers a swim in cool, fresh water surrounded by dense vegetation. It's a welcome way to decompress after an intense day among the burial sites. Locals come here for a family swim.

visits.text: Transit day in Makassar before the flight to Selayar. Use the time to explore what you haven't seen yet: Somba Opu Street, a kilometre-long shopping strip entirely dedicated to South Sulawesi crafts — Bugis filigree silver jewellery, geometrically patterned silk textiles, wood carvings and ceramics. It's the best address in town for authentic souvenirs.

Akkarena Beach, 5 km south of the city centre, offers a moment of relaxation before departure. A seaside stroll, coconut vendors and a sunset over the Makassar Strait. It's a leisure spot for local families — relaxed and thoroughly local in feel.

activities.text: Evening or late-afternoon flight from Makassar (UPG) to Selayar Island (MLG). The flight takes roughly 30 to 45 minutes. Wings Air operates this route several times a week — check schedules in advance as they vary by season.

map_keywords: Selayar Island Benteng village South Sulawesi

visits.text: Selayar is a north-south elongated island of 2,000 km² in South Sulawesi, separated from the mainland by a narrow strait. The island sees few foreign tourists and offers a rural, peaceful side of Sulawesi: rice fields, coconut palms, fishermen and early-morning local markets. The capital, Benteng, concentrates the administrative life and the handful of hotels, but it's the marine environment that makes the island's reputation.

Off the coast of Selayar lies Takabonerate Atoll — the third-largest coral atoll in the world and the largest in Asia, covering 220,000 hectares of reef. Its waters harbour some of the most diverse corals in the Flores Sea. Boat excursions from Selayar allow for a full day of snorkelling and diving in waters of exceptional clarity. Count on about 2 hours each way by boat from Benteng.

activities.text: Snorkelling on the fringing reefs close to the island, accessible from Baloiya beach or from the coastal fishing villages. Selayar's reefs are far less crowded than those of Bunaken or Wakatobi, and some remain completely intact. It's your chance to dive in near-total solitude alongside clownfish, stingrays and schools of blue surgeonfish.

map_keywords: Bajo sea gypsy village Wakatobi stilt houses

visits.text: Welcome to Wakatobi — the name is an acronym of the four main islands: Wangi-Wangi, Kaledupa, Tomia and Binongko. This national marine park covering 1.39 million hectares was established in 1996 and designated a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve in 2012. Explorer Jacques Cousteau reportedly called it an "underwater nirvana". The numbers are staggering: over 750 coral species, 942 recorded fish species — roughly five times the coral diversity of Hawaii — and 50 charted dive sites. Average visibility is 35 metres. With no rivers or streams carrying sediment, the water stays perfectly clear year-round. Wakatobi boasts Indonesia's largest barrier reef — the second-largest in Asia after Australia's Great Barrier Reef.

The Bajo stilt villages in Wangi-Wangi Bay are one of the archipelago's most powerful cultural images. The Bajo (or Bajau) people, often called "sea gypsies", have lived in these houses suspended above the water for generations. They are celebrated in the scientific world for their extraordinary breath-holding ability — some can hold their breath for over 13 minutes and dive to depths beyond 60 metres. A 2018 study found that their spleens are naturally 50% larger than those of their land-dwelling neighbours, enabling them to store more oxygen.

activities.text: First dive at Roma Reef, one of the most photographed sites in Wakatobi. This pinnacle coral site features two giant 300-year-old coral disc formations known as "The Rose" — two massive coral flowers several metres across. Around them, a dense parade of life: leopard groupers, eagle rays, schools of fusiliers and blue surgeonfish. The site is suitable for all levels. Book as soon as you arrive with operators on Wangi-Wangi.

practical_info.booking: Operators available on all four islands

visits.text: Tomia, the third island of the archipelago, is widely regarded as offering the best diving in Wakatobi. Sites like Fan Garden — a wall draped in huge sea fans and soft corals, swept by schools of bumphead parrotfish — and Magnifica — a gently sloping reef ideal for drift diving among snappers, skipjack and giant barracuda — are regularly cited among the ten best dive sites in Southeast Asia. Tomia is home to an internationally renowned dive resort that provides exclusive access to certain sites.

Kaledupa hosts the atoll of the same name — one of the most pristine coral formations in the park. A boat tour around the atoll offers chances to spot dolphins (bottlenose, Risso's and spinner dolphins are regulars in these waters) and, with luck, sperm whales and pilot whales migrating through the depths of the Banda Sea.

activities.text: For experienced divers, The Blade — a cluster of seamounts spread across 200 metres in a rarely visited area — is one of Wakatobi's most intense dives. The site is only accessible in good weather and favourable current conditions. Hammerhead sharks, reef sharks, dense schools of giant trevally and rare nudibranchs make every dive here a different experience. Check with operators on the day's conditions.

In the evening, a night dive in Wakatobi's shallow corals to witness the mating ritual of the mandarinfish (Synchiropus splendidus) — a ceremony that takes place every evening at sunset on specific sites. The mandarinfish is among the most colourful in the world — electric blue, green and orange — and only emerges in the darkness. Operators who know these specific sites will guide your night outing.

practical_info.note: Exact time given by the guide depending on the season

visits.text: If you have time before your return flight, Binongko — the fourth and most remote island of the archipelago — is the least touristic of all four. It's nicknamed "The Island of Blacksmiths" because its artisans have been practising traditional knife and tool forging for centuries. A visit to a local smithy lets you watch techniques passed down through generations. Binongko also has hard-to-reach white-sand beaches and pristine reefs.

Final dive or snorkelling session from Wangi-Wangi's fringing reef. This so-called "House Reef", accessible by wading from shore, drops off into a vertical wall just 50 m from the coast. Green turtles, stingrays, leopard groupers and nudibranchs of every kind congregate here in a single dive. The sunset over the Banda Sea from your accommodation's terrace wraps up the journey.

activities.text: Departure from Matahora Airport (WNI) on Wangi-Wangi towards Makassar or Kendari, with a connection to your final destination. Flights operate several times a week from Wangi-Wangi. If connections don't line up, an extra night in Makassar lets you plan the return journey at a comfortable pace.

seo.h1_title: Sulawesi: Bunaken Reefs, Toraja Highlands and Wakatobi Lagoons in 20 Days
seo.intro_paragraph: Bunaken packs 390 coral species and 2,000 fish species onto vertical walls plunging 1,800 metres — one of Indonesia's first marine parks since 1991. At Tangkoko, the spectral tarsier (80 grams, eyes bigger than its brain) hunts at nightfall in the last intact forest of the Minahasa. In Tana Toraja, families keep their dead at home — dressed, fed, treated as if merely ill — sometimes for years, before burying them in grand cliff-side ceremonies. At Wakatobi, Jacques Cousteau called it an "underwater nirvana" upon discovering its 750 coral species.
seo.seo_hook: Bunaken's coral walls, Tangkoko's tarsiers, Tana Toraja's funeral rites and Wakatobi's record-breaking reefs — 20 days of Sulawesi at full throttle.
featured_list: Bunaken: 390 coral species on vertical walls plunging to 1,800 m
Tangkoko: last refuge of the endemic crested black macaque (5,500 remaining)
Tana Toraja: Rambu Solo funerals with hundreds of buffaloes sacrificed
Wakatobi: Indonesia's largest barrier reef, a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve

highlights: Bunaken: vertical coral walls plunging to 1,800 m, 390 coral species and 2,000 fish species — a marine park since 1991
Tangkoko: the spectral tarsier, world's smallest primate (80 g), and the endemic crested black macaque with only 5,500 individuals left on Earth
Tana Toraja: the Rambu Solo funeral ceremonies (June–August) with buffalo sacrifices, cliff-side tau-tau burial sites and boat-roofed tongkonan houses
Wakatobi: 750 coral species, 942 fish species, UNESCO Biosphere Reserve since 2012 — Indonesia's largest barrier reef

itineraries.title: The Grand Sumatra Tour: Orangutans, Lake Toba, Mentawai Shamans and Pagaralam Megaliths
itineraries.subtitle: From the wild orangutans of Gunung Leuser to the tattooed shamans of Siberut, through the volcanic Lake Toba and the mysterious megaliths of Pagaralam — sixteen days across the untouched heart of Sumatra.
itineraries.notes: The Padang–Siberut ferry (D9) runs roughly twice a week (6 to 12 hours crossing); check schedules before departing. The Padang–Bengkulu flight (D11→D12) is the only reasonable option — the overland route is long and grueling. The Rafflesia arnoldii near Bengkulu blooms only a few days a year; ask local guides about current blooms. The Gunung Dempo trek (D14, activity) is optional and requires a 2 AM start — incompatible with the 7-hour drive to Jambi on the same day; plan to push the Jambi departure to the following morning if you do the trek. The itin_id 'medan-bintan-25j' is kept for backward compatibility with the existing database.

visits.text: Start with the Tjong A Fie Mansion (Jl. Ahmad Yani No.105), a 35-room residence spanning 8,000 m² built in 1895 by a Hakka merchant who arrived in Sumatra with nothing and became the island's first millionaire. Tjong A Fie (1860–1921) landed in Medan in 1875 with a few silver coins sewn into his belt, built an empire in tobacco and plantations, employed over 10,000 workers, and personally financed the construction of both the Masjid Raya mosque and the Maimun Palace. Inside, dark imported woodwork, mother-of-pearl marquetry, Art Nouveau chandeliers and Taoist shrines collide — a cultural collision that says everything about colonial Medan. Allow an hour and take the included guided tour.

Steps away, the Maimun Palace (Istana Maimun) is the residence of the Deli Sultanate, built between 1887 and 1891 by Sultan Ma'mun Al Rashid Perkasa Alamyah to plans drawn by the Dutch architect Theodoor Van Erp. Its 2,772 m² blend Malay heritage, Mughal architecture, Spanish furniture and Italian ornamentation — a colonial syncretism you won't find anywhere else in Sumatra. Only the throne room is open to the public; it's here you'll see the royal kris and a collection of court ceremony photographs frozen in time.

The Grand Mosque Masjid Raya Al Mashun, just across from the palace, was jointly funded by the sultanate and Tjong A Fie himself — a telling detail about the alliances of the era. Built between 1906 and 1909, it combines Middle Eastern architecture, Indian influences and Spanish elements in an octagonal floor plan with four cardinal wings. Remove your shoes at the entrance and step inside: the mosaics and arabesque work around the mihrab are well worth the stop.

Finish with the Sri Mariamman Hindu Temple (Jl. H. Zainul Arifin), founded in 1884 — Medan's oldest. Its multi-tiered Dravidian gopuram covered in colorful deities looks wildly out of place in this commercial neighborhood. It's a reminder of the large Tamil community brought here by the Dutch to work the 19th-century plantations. Entry is free; if a ceremony is underway, stay discreetly to one side.

activities.text: Stroll along Jalan Ahmad Yani in the historic Kesawan quarter, the colonial-era artery where Dutch facades, Chinese signboards and modern cafes sit side by side. This is where Medan reveals its multicultural DNA: Batak, Malay, Chinese, Tamil and Dutch have all left their mark within the same 500 meters. In the evening, head to the Merdeka Walk or the street stalls around Merdeka Square for local food — the nasi goreng batak here is nothing like Bali's version.

visits.text: The North Sumatra Museum (Museum Negeri Sumatera Utara) deserves a full morning. Housed in a building shaped like a traditional rumah adat, it spans everything from the island's earliest Neolithic civilizations to the Hindu-Buddhist and later Islamic kingdoms. The sections on the Batak Toba, Karo and Mandailing peoples provide essential context before heading into the Highlands. Allow 90 minutes.

Take a walk through the Indian quarter (Little India) around Jalan Semarang: a dense cluster of textiles, spices and temples that recalls the decades when Medan was a city of Tamil coolies employed in rubber and tobacco plantations. The Shri Mariamman Hindoo temple (not to be confused with yesterday's) and the storefronts of sari shops and saffron vendors give this corner of town a character all its own.

activities.text: Dive into Petisah Market, Medan's busiest. Multiple levels of stalls overflow with exotic fruit, Batak spices, grilled meats and clothing — arrive early in the morning to catch the deliveries and the haggling. This is where you'll find fresh durian at reasonable prices: Medan is reputed to have the best durian in Sumatra, a local specialty that residents eat in the open street with unabashed pride.

Prepare for tomorrow's departure to Bukit Lawang: check with your accommodation about transport (roughly 3 hours by road) and book your jungle guide in advance. Local operators offer one-day or multi-night treks into Gunung Leuser National Park — an overnight trek is highly recommended to boost your chances of spotting orangutans away from the busy areas.

map_keywords: Gunung Leuser National Park Bukit Lawang

visits.text: Bukit Lawang is the gateway village to Gunung Leuser National Park, one of only two wild areas on Earth where the Sumatran orangutan (Pongo abelii) still roams free. The park covers 7,927 km² across the provinces of Aceh and North Sumatra and was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2004 — with an "in danger" tag added in 2011 due to deforestation. A rehabilitation center was established in 1973 to reintroduce orangutans rescued from captivity back into the wild. Settle into your riverside guesthouse on the Bohorok River and familiarize yourself with the park rules: never touch the animals, keep a minimum distance, and never feed them.

In the late afternoon, do a reconnaissance at the park entrance and information center to understand the accessible zones and the species present. The park is home to 130 mammal species including the Sumatran orangutan, Sumatran tiger, Sumatran elephant, Sumatran rhinoceros and sun bear — plus 285 bird species including the rhinoceros hornbill. Statistically, your chances of encountering a tiger or rhinoceros are vanishingly small; orangutans and Thomas leaf monkeys, on the other hand, are very much around.

activities.text: First jungle sortie at dusk from the village outskirts: the semi-wild orangutans living on the forest edge often come down in the late afternoon. It's a gentle introduction before tomorrow's full-day trek. Soak in the falling darkness on the Bohorok River — the sound of the nocturnal jungle, with gibbons calling and hornbills settling in, is an experience in itself.

map_keywords: Orangutan trek Gunung Leuser Bukit Lawang

visits.text: Full day trekking in Gunung Leuser National Park with a certified guide — it's mandatory and essential to the experience. The park has been described as the largest wilderness area in Southeast Asia, and unmarked trails vanish rapidly in the dense vegetation. A 6- to 7-hour outing will take you far enough past the busy zones to dramatically improve your chances of observing wild orangutans in their natural behavior — not begging for food, but foraging for fruit, building their nests or swinging through the canopy.

activities.text: River tubing down the Bohorok in the late afternoon: local guides provide inner tubes for riding the rapids from the upper reaches of the park. It's the most enjoyable return you could wish for after a long day of hiking — the water is cool, the forested banks slide past, and monkeys watch you from the branches. Count on 90 minutes to 2 hours depending on the starting point.

visits.text: Parapat is the town on the eastern shore of Lake Toba and the embarkation point for Samosir Island. Lake Toba itself is a staggering sight: 100 km long, 35 km wide, 505 meters at its deepest — the largest volcanic lake in the world, formed roughly 74,000 years ago by a supervolcanic eruption so powerful that some scientists believe it triggered a global volcanic winter and halved the human population. The fact that this cataclysmic site is now covered with terraced rice paddies, Batak villages and cloud reflections on still water feels utterly surreal.

From the Parapat lookout, spot Samosir Island in the center of the lake — a 650 km² landmass (roughly the size of Singapore) connected to the mainland by an isthmus cut through with an artificial canal. Its peak rises to 1,630 meters above the lake surface, or 2,600 meters above sea level — making it technically an island in a lake, on an island, on a volcano. Hard to beat in terms of improbable geography.

activities.text: Arrival in the late afternoon after the long drive from Bukit Lawang — count on 4 to 5 hours on the road. Settle in at Parapat and locate the morning ferry departure for Samosir (frequent crossings to Tuk Tuk, about 1 hour). The evening by the lake, with the lights of the far-shore villages reflecting on the water, is the best possible introduction to why the Batak call this lake the origin of the world.

map_keywords: Batak Museum Huta Bolon Simanindo Samosir

visits.text: Tuk Tuk, the peninsula on Samosir's east shore, is the island's tourist hub — but a few kilometers inland is all it takes to reach traditional villages where rumah adat (Batak longhouses with saddle-shaped roofs and curved gables) shelter families whose genealogies stretch back 18 generations. Start with the village of Ambarita and its tribunal stones: an open-air stone courtyard where King Siallagan dispensed justice centuries ago, complete with stone seats for the accused and a chopping block for the condemned. Stark and chilling.

The tomb of King Sidabutar is one of the best-preserved royal Batak tombs on Samosir — a sculpted sarcophagus shaped like an overturned boat, adorned with ancestral heads and geometric friezes. The Batak Toba built their tombs so that ancestral spirits could watch over the living: every architectural detail carries meaning. Mount Pusuk Buhit, visible from Samosir, is considered the mythical birthplace of the Batak — the home of Si Raja Batak, ancestor of the entire people.

The Huta Bolon Museum in Simanindo, in the north of the island, features a royal rumah adat over 140 years old and a collection of Batak crafts — ulos textiles with complex geometric iconography, two-stringed hasapi lutes, ritual carvings. Tortor dance performances are held on most mornings.

activities.text: If you have the energy, hike up to Lake Sidihoni, perched on the Samosir plateau at 900 meters — a lake within an island within a lake, surrounded by rice paddies and total silence. The round trip from Tuk Tuk takes half a day. In the late afternoon, settle into one of the lakeside cafes at Tuk Tuk and order a glass of tuak, the Batak palm wine served at every significant occasion — weddings, funerals, Sundays after church.

visits.text: Bukittinggi means literally "high hill" in Malay — the town sits at 930 meters in the Minangkabau Highlands, 90 km northeast of Padang, at the foot of volcanoes Singgalang (dormant) and Marapi (active). Its landmark is the Jam Gadang clock tower, built by the Dutch in 1926, whose roof was restyled under Japanese occupation and then converted again to a curved Minangkabau-style roof at independence — three chapters of history told by a single building. Grab a table at the terrace across the square and watch the plaza come alive.

Sianok Canyon (Ngarai Sianok), a 10-minute walk from the center, is a 15-km gorge sliced through volcanic rock — locals compare it to a miniature Grand Canyon. From the Taman Panorama on the cliff edge, the view drops to a jungle floor threaded by a silver river, with the houses of Kota Gadang (famous for its goldsmiths) clinging to the opposite wall. Beneath your feet, tunnels dug by prisoners of war under Japanese occupation run for kilometers — part of the network is open to visitors.

Fort de Kock (Benteng Fort de Kock) was built in 1825 by Captain Bouer on orders from Governor Hendrik Merkus de Kock, at the height of the Padri War (1821–1837) — an internal Minangkabau conflict between Islamic reformers and guardians of adat tradition that the Dutch exploited to gain a foothold. Little of the original fortification survives, but the surrounding park commands a panorama over the town and the Limpapeh suspension bridge linking the hilltop to the nearby zoo and museum.

activities.text: Wander through Pasar Atas (the upper market), Bukittinggi's liveliest, for a first taste of Minangkabau cuisine: rendang (beef slow-cooked in coconut milk and spices, named "the world's most delicious food" by CNN in 2011), gulai otak (brain curry), lado mudo (fresh green chili). Sit down for a full meal at a traditional warung — the dishes are laid out on the table and you pay only for what you eat.

visits.text: Descend into Sianok Canyon to the village of Kota Gadang, renowned since the 17th century for its goldsmiths and embroiderers. The trail along the gorge floor follows the river for about an hour's walk — one of the finest footpaths in West Sumatra. Kota Gadang's silver filigree jewelry adorned Minangkabau sultanas and notables for centuries; you'll still find artisan workshops open to visitors.

The Rumah Adat Baanjuang Museum, on Fort de Kock hill, is housed in a rumah gadang (a grand house with buffalo-horn rooflines) built in 1934 by the Dutch governor. It's the province's oldest museum and holds one of the finest collections of Minangkabau craftsmanship: songket textiles, gamelan instruments, ceremonial weapons and early 20th-century photographs that freeze Minangkabau society before its transformation.

activities.text: Day trip to Lake Maninjau (about 40 km), a volcanic caldera with blue-green waters at 460 meters above sea level. The descent via the road of 44 hairpin bends (Kelok 44) from the heights is a spectacle in itself — the lake appears suddenly after each turn, ringed by forested hills. An hour's boat ride at sunset is the single best memory you'll bring back from Bukittinggi.

visits.text: From Padang (2 hours by road from Bukittinggi), take the ferry to Siberut Island — the largest of the Mentawai Islands, 4,480 km², designated a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve and located 150 km off the west coast of Sumatra. The crossing takes 6 to 12 hours depending on the boat; slow ferries depart in the evening and arrive in the morning. Siberut is home to the Mentawai, nicknamed the "flower people" — one of the last Neolithic populations in Indonesia, carrying on a tradition stretching back 3,000 years. They know neither rice cultivation, nor ironwork, nor weaving: their life revolves around hunting, fishing and forest gathering.

The basic social unit of the Mentawai is not the village but the uma, a large communal wooden longhouse on stilts shared by 5 to 10 families from the same clan. It serves simultaneously as home, courthouse, ceremonial space and temple — decisions are reached by consensus after meetings that can run through the entire night. Since Suharto's fall in 1998, families have begun rebuilding umas in the forest, after decades of forced sedentarization during which the Indonesian army burned these houses down to "modernize" the population.

activities.text: First encounter with the sikerei — the Mentawai shamans — accompanied by a local guide.
